positions.argument: The execution chapters confirm every instruction was followed. The reader witnesses total obedience — which is the theological point after the golden calf's total disobedience. The length is the message.

positions.label: Two independent sources combined
positions.argument: The instruction (P source) and execution accounts were originally independent documents combined by a later editor. The repetition reflects source combination rather than intentional literary structure.

title: The Ark: Furniture or Throne?
question: The Ark: Furniture or Throne?

positions.label: Divine footstool/throne
positions.argument: The Ark is the footstool of the invisible divine throne — God is enthroned above the cherubim (Ps 80:1; 99:1). The mercy seat is the meeting point between footstool and invisible enthroned presence.

positions.label: Container for the covenant document
positions.argument: The Ark is primarily a storage chest for the covenant tablets. Its sacredness derives from what it contains.

title: The Materials Census: Historical Record or Theological Testimony?
question: The Materials Census: Historical Record or Theological Testimony?

positions.label: Historical record
positions.argument: The census is a genuine financial record consistent with ancient Near Eastern temple building records. Egyptian and Mesopotamian records routinely documented materials used.

positions.label: Theological idealization
positions.argument: The numbers are too precise to be genuine records — a theological statement about perfect obedience, not an accountant's ledger.

title: 'Just as the LORD commanded': How Many Times in Ch.39?
question: 'Just as the LORD commanded': How Many Times in Ch.39?

positions.label: Seven times specifically in ch.39
positions.argument: The phrase appears seven times in ch.39 (vv.1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31) — the creation number. The sevenfold completion of the priestly garments mirrors the seven days of creation.

positions.label: Approximately sixteen times in chs.39–40
positions.argument: Counting all instances in the execution section, the phrase appears approximately sixteen times. The cumulative effect confirms total obedience.

title: Why Does Exodus End with the Glory and Not with Entry into Canaan?
question: Why Does Exodus End with the Glory and Not with Entry into Canaan?

positions.label: The Exodus's goal is divine dwelling, not territorial possession
positions.argument: The book ends where its purpose statement said it would (29:45: 'I will dwell among the Israelites'). The land is the next chapter; the covenant goal — divine presence among the covenant people — is achieved here.

positions.label: Editorial decision to separate Exodus from Numbers
positions.argument: The Pentateuch's division into five books is a later editorial decision. The natural narrative continuation is the journey to Canaan. Exodus 40 is an artificial stopping point.

title: Source and Date of The Burnt Offering
question: Source and Date of The Burnt Offering

positions.label: Documentary hypothesis (Wellhausen, Noth)
positions.proponents: Assigns The Burnt Offering to the Priestly (P) source, dated to the post-exilic period (6th–5th c. BC) on the basis of vocabulary, theology, and redaction-critical analysis.
positions.argument: This view treats the Levitical legislation as crystallised priestly tradition rather than Mosaic prescription.

positions.label: Unified authorship (Milgrom, Wenham, conservative scholarship)
positions.proponents: Argues that the detailed ANE parallels, archaic vocabulary, and internal coherence support a second-millennium date for The Burnt Offering.
positions.argument: Milgrom’s detailed comparison with ANE ritual texts demonstrates the antiquity and coherence of the material regardless of the dating question.

title: Authorship and Date of Deuteronomy
question: Authorship and Date of Deuteronomy

positions.label: Critical scholarship: Josian composition (de Wette, Wellhausen)
positions.argument: This view treats the Mosaic attribution as a literary fiction and explains the book’s similarities with 7th-century Assyrian treaty forms — though the Hittite parallels are structurally closer.

positions.label: Mosaic authorship / early composition (Kline, Craigie, evangelical scholarship)
positions.argument: Craigie and Kline argue that the treaty-form evidence makes a late date structurally implausible. The “prophet like Moses” passage (18:15) is best read as a genuine prediction, not a post-exilic retrojection.

title: Historicity of the Conquest
question: Historicity of the Conquest

positions.label: Military conquest (Albright, Kitchen)
positions.proponents: W.F. Albright, K. Kitchen, R. Hess
positions.argument: Archaeological destruction layers at Hazor, Lachish, and Bethel support a late Bronze Age conquest. The book reflects historical memory of a swift, unified military campaign c.1400 BC.

positions.label: Gradual infiltration (Alt, Noth)
positions.argument: Settlement was peaceful and incremental — pastoral nomads gradually settled alongside existing populations. Joshua's conquest narrative is theological, not historical.

positions.label: Peasant revolt (Mendenhall, Gottwald)
positions.argument: Canaanite underclass revolted against city-state rulers, later mythologised as external conquest. Archaeology shows cultural continuity, not destruction.

title: The Date of Ruth: Pre-Exilic or Post-Exilic Composition?
question: The Date of Ruth: Pre-Exilic or Post-Exilic Composition?

positions.label: Pre-exilic, possibly Solomonic era
positions.argument: The archaic legal practices described (the sandal-removal ceremony, the kinsman-redeemer institution) fit the pre-monarchic or early monarchic period. The book's positive portrayal of Moabites would be unusual in the post-exilic period (cf. Ezra-Nehemiah's harsh stance). The genealogy ending with David (4:17–22) suggests composition shortly after David's rise.

positions.label: Post-exilic, composed as a counter-narrative to Ezra-Nehemiah
positions.argument: Ruth's positive portrayal of a Moabite woman and her integration into Israel counters the Ezra-Nehemiah programme of expelling foreign wives. The book is a literary argument for inclusion, composed after Ezra's reforms to challenge their rigidity.

positions.label: Persian period, but not as Ezra-Nehemiah polemic
positions.argument: The language shows some late features but the legal customs appear archaic. The book may have been composed or heavily edited in the Persian period without being directly polemical.

title: The Go'el Institution: Levirate Marriage or Property Redemption?
question: The Go'el Institution: Levirate Marriage or Property Redemption?

positions.label: Property redemption primarily
positions.argument: The go'el in the Pentateuchal law is primarily a property-redemption institution (Lev 25:25–55). The levirate marriage law (Deut 25:5–10) is a separate institution applying specifically to a dead brother's widow. Ruth conflates or adapts both institutions in ways that don't exactly match either.

positions.label: A unified institution of family rescue
positions.argument: Ruth presents a coherent institution in which property redemption and family preservation (including widow-care) belong together. The go'el who redeems property without marrying the widow leaves the family's future unprotected. Boaz understands the institution holistically — not as two separate legal obligations but as one act of family redemption.

title: The Morality of the Threshing Floor Scene: Naomi's Plan and Ruth's Action
question: The Morality of the Threshing Floor Scene: Naomi's Plan and Ruth's Action

positions.label: Entirely proper: a legal marriage proposal
positions.argument: The scene is structured as a formal go'el marriage petition. The uncovering of feet, the lying at his feet, the request to spread his kanaf — all are elements of a recognised legal proposal, not a sexual encounter. The narrator's silence about any impropriety is evidence of propriety. Boaz's immediate concern for Ruth's reputation (3:14) confirms the scene was understood as legally, not sexually, charged.

positions.label: Ambiguous: the text deliberately leaves the night ambiguous
positions.argument: The scene deliberately uses vocabulary with sexual connotations (feet can be a euphemism, the darkness, the secrecy). The ambiguity is the point — the narrator leaves open what happened in order to focus on Ruth and Boaz's character: whatever happened, both acted honourably.

title: Ruth as Typology of Christ's Redemption: How Direct is the Connection?
question: Ruth as Typology of Christ's Redemption: How Direct is the Connection?

positions.label: Direct Christological typology
positions.argument: Boaz is a type of Christ in the go'el institution: the kinsman-redeemer who voluntarily accepts the full cost of redemption that the law requires, rescuing the widow (the helpless) at personal cost, restoring the inheritance, preserving the family name. The parallels are structural and precise: Christ redeems not from compulsion but from voluntary love; he accepts the cost that others decline; his redemption is complete.

positions.label: Canonical resonance without strict typology
positions.argument: The go'el institution anticipates redemption theology without Boaz being a type of Christ in the technical typological sense. The connections are thematic: redemption through a kinsman, voluntary cost, inclusion of the Gentile. But the book's primary theological concern is hesed as a human and divine virtue, not Christological anticipation.

title: Who Was Sheshbazzar?
question: Who Was Sheshbazzar?

positions.label: Sheshbazzar = Zerubbabel
positions.argument: Both are called "governor" and credited with laying the foundation. Possibly the same person with different names.

positions.label: Sheshbazzar ≠ Zerubbabel
positions.argument: Different genealogies. Sheshbazzar may be Shenazzar (1 Chr 3:18). He disappears; Zerubbabel completes the work. Two generations, two leaders.

title: The Chronological Displacement of 4:6–23
question: The Chronological Displacement of 4:6–23

positions.label: Thematic arrangement
positions.argument: The letters to Xerxes (v.6) and Artaxerxes (vv.7–23) postdate the temple completion. They are inserted here to collect all opposition episodes before the resolution in chapters 5–6.

positions.label: Strictly chronological
positions.argument: The text should be read as sequential. Ahasuerus and Artaxerxes may be alternative names for earlier Persian kings.

title: The Ethics of the Mass Divorce
question: The Ethics of the Mass Divorce

positions.label: Justified by covenant priority
positions.argument: The survival of the covenant community required radical action. Syncretism had caused the exile; preventing its recurrence justified the painful measure. The marriages themselves violated Torah.

positions.label: Morally troubling
positions.argument: The text itself hints at discomfort: children are mentioned, four men object, the book ends without celebration. The narrator does not whitewash the cost. Malachi 2:16 ("I hate divorce") stands in tension with Ezra 10.

title: What Destruction Does 1:3 Describe?
question: What Destruction Does 1:3 Describe?

positions.label: Original Babylonian destruction (586 BC)
positions.argument: The walls destroyed by Nebuchadnezzar had never been rebuilt. Nehemiah’s grief is at the ongoing state of affairs.

positions.label: Recent destruction under Artaxerxes (c.448 BC)
positions.argument: Ezra 4:23 records a forced halt. A partial rebuilding was torn down again. Nehemiah’s grief is at a fresh setback, not century-old ruins.

title: Nehemiah’s Methods
question: Nehemiah’s Methods

positions.label: Justified by context
positions.argument: The severity of the crisis demanded severe action. Hair-pulling and threats were within the cultural norms of ancient Near Eastern judicial authority. Nehemiah acted as governor with legitimate coercive power.

positions.label: Troublingly violent
positions.argument: Physical violence against community members raises ethical questions regardless of cultural context. Ezra’s approach (personal grief, prayer) may model a more sustainable reform pattern.

title: The Personification of Wisdom in Proverbs 1–9: Poetic Device or Ontological Reality?
question: The Personification of Wisdom in Proverbs 1–9: Poetic Device or Ontological Reality?

positions.label: Poetic personification only
positions.argument: Lady Wisdom is a literary device — personified wisdom as the ideal that the parent urges the son to pursue. The figure has no independent ontological status; it is a rhetorical vehicle for the book's ethical teaching. The background is ancient Near Eastern wisdom literature's tendency to personify abstract qualities.

positions.label: Pre-existent cosmic Wisdom with ontological status
positions.argument: Prov 8:22–31 presents Wisdom as present at creation, 'beside God' as a master craftsman/delight. This is not mere rhetorical flourish but a claim about a real divine attribute that has quasi-personal status. The NT's identification of Christ as the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:30; Col 1:15–20) develops this as Christological: Wisdom in Prov 8 is the pre-existent Son.

positions.label: Both: literary figure pointing to divine reality
positions.argument: Lady Wisdom is simultaneously a literary device AND a pointer toward divine reality. The personification is real as personification and significant as theology. The question of ontological status is not the right question — the text uses the figure to make claims about the character of divine wisdom that the literal-or-figurative binary cannot capture.

title: The 'Strange Woman': Literal or Symbolic?
question: The 'Strange Woman': Literal or Symbolic?

positions.label: Primarily a literal warning against adultery
positions.argument: The strange woman is first of all a real woman — a married woman who seduces young men. The warnings are practical and specific: her house leads to death (v.18), her paths to the shades (v.19). The wisdom poems use her as a concrete example of the folly that destroys life, not merely as a symbol.

positions.label: An anti-type to Lady Wisdom, primarily symbolic
positions.argument: Lady Wisdom and Dame Folly/the Strange Woman are literary doubles — both are women, both address young men, both offer different visions of the good life. The Strange Woman is the symbolic inverse of Wisdom, and her 'foreignness' is primarily ideological (outside the covenant order) rather than ethnic.

positions.label: Both literal and symbolic simultaneously
positions.argument: The Strange Woman is simultaneously a real social danger (adultery with a married woman) and a symbolic figure representing all that is anti-wisdom, anti-covenant, anti-life. The literal and symbolic meanings reinforce each other: the specific sin of adultery is also an instance of the general category of covenant defection.

title: Is Proverbs 3:5–6 Anti-Intellectual?
question: Is Proverbs 3:5–6 Anti-Intellectual?

positions.label: Not anti-intellectual: submission to revelation, not abandonment of reason
positions.argument: 'Lean not on your own understanding' prohibits the autonomous self-sufficiency that treats human reason as the final court of appeal, not the use of human reason itself. Solomon, who exemplifies Proverbs' wisdom, is the OT's greatest intellectual. The proverb calls for epistemic humility, not intellectual abdication.

positions.label: A tension with intellectual inquiry that must be held
positions.argument: The verse does create a genuine tension with the Enlightenment ideal of autonomous reason. The biblical wisdom tradition places human inquiry within the framework of divine revelation — not as a constraint on thought but as its necessary condition. This is a different epistemology from Cartesian autonomy and should be recognised as such.

title: 'Heart' in Proverbs: Intellect, Will, or Emotion?
question: 'Heart' in Proverbs: Intellect, Will, or Emotion?

positions.label: Primarily intellectual: the thinking/reasoning faculty
positions.argument: In Hebrew anthropology, lev/levav (heart) is primarily the seat of thought, reason, and decision-making. 'As he thinks in his heart' (Prov 23:7); 'a wise heart' (Prov 16:23). The heart reasons, plans, and decides. To 'guard the heart' is to guard one's thinking and willing.

positions.label: The whole inner person: intellect, will, and emotion integrated
positions.argument: The OT does not map cleanly onto the Greek intellect/emotion distinction. The heart (lev) encompasses all that is 'inside' — thought, desire, will, feeling — as an integrated whole. Guarding the heart means maintaining the integrated inner person in wisdom-orientation, not just managing thoughts.

positions.label: Primarily volitional: the decision-making centre
positions.argument: The frequency of 'the heart decides/plans/purposes' language (Prov 16:1, 9; 19:21) suggests the primary locus is decisional — the heart is where choices are made. Guarding it means protecting the decision-making centre from corruption.

title: Does Proverbs 5 Endorse Sexual Desire or Merely Regulate It?
question: Does Proverbs 5 Endorse Sexual Desire or Merely Regulate It?

positions.label: Regulation of desire, not celebration
positions.argument: The chapter's primary concern is protection from adultery. The positive commands (rejoice in your wife, be intoxicated with her love) are remedies for temptation rather than celebrations of sexuality in themselves. The chapter is prudential: use marital love to avoid the greater danger.

positions.label: Genuine celebration of marital sexuality
positions.argument: The erotic language of vv.15–19 is not merely functional (use marriage to avoid adultery) but genuinely celebratory. The 'intoxication' metaphor, the doe/deer simile, the 'at all times' qualifier all signal that marital sexual joy is itself a good to be pursued, not merely a prophylactic. The chapter integrates covenantal faithfulness with erotic celebration.

title: The Seven Abominations: A Moral Hierarchy or Illustrative List?
question: The Seven Abominations: A Moral Hierarchy or Illustrative List?

positions.label: Illustrative, not exhaustive or hierarchical
positions.argument: The seven items are representative examples of the character dispositions the LORD finds detestable — not an exhaustive moral catalogue or a ranked hierarchy. The numerical structure (six/seven) signals completeness by example rather than completeness by enumeration.

positions.label: The final item is the climax: communal sin is the greatest
positions.argument: The X/X+1 structure places the final item (stirring up discord among brothers) as the climactic abomination. The movement from individual sins (lying, murder, deceit) to the communal sin of divisiveness suggests that covenant community destruction is worse than any individual sin — a counter-intuitive priority that Proverbs articulates in several places.

title: The Seductress of Proverbs 7: Moral Warning or Misogynist Portrait?
question: The Seductress of Proverbs 7: Moral Warning or Misogynist Portrait?

positions.label: A genuine moral warning, not anti-woman
positions.argument: The text addresses a specific social danger — the adulteress who preys on young men — and describes her methods with psychological precision. The father's purpose is protective, not misogynist. Lady Wisdom (ch.8) is the counter-figure, and she is also a woman. Proverbs does not associate women with danger but associates a specific moral choice (adultery) with danger for both parties.

positions.label: A gendered discourse that encodes patriarchal anxiety
positions.argument: The consistent portrayal of the threatening 'other' as a woman in chs.1–9 encodes a patriarchal anxiety about female sexuality and agency. The implied reader is male; danger is coded female; wisdom is domesticated as 'sister' or 'wife.' The gendered structure of chs.1–9 reflects the social arrangements of ancient patriarchy more than eternal moral truth.

positions.label: Both concerns are legitimate and need holding together
positions.argument: The text has a genuine moral concern (adultery destroys) and a genuine gendered structure (the implied student is male, the threat is female). Both can be acknowledged: the moral concern is true regardless of the gendered framing; the gendered framing reflects its historical context without invalidating the moral point. Contemporary application requires both taking the moral concern seriously and reading the gendered framing contextually.

title: Is the Wisdom of Proverbs 8 the Pre-Existent Christ?
question: Is the Wisdom of Proverbs 8 the Pre-Existent Christ?

positions.label: Yes: explicit Christological anticipation
positions.argument: Prov 8 is the OT's fullest pre-incarnate portrait of the second person of the Trinity. The NT is explicit: John 1:1–3 (In the beginning was the Word... through him all things were made) and Col 1:15–17 (the firstborn of all creation... all things were created through him) develop the cosmic wisdom of Prov 8 into Christology. 'Wisdom' in Prov 8 is not identical to Christ but is the divine attribute that becomes incarnate in Christ.

positions.label: A personified divine attribute, not a separate person
positions.argument: Wisdom in Prov 8 is the personification of God's own creative intelligence — a literary and theological device, not a separate divine hypostasis. The NT's appropriation of the language to describe Christ is a legitimate Christian theological development, but it reads back into Proverbs a Trinitarian framework the text does not itself contain.

positions.label: The figure points toward but does not constitute Christology
positions.argument: Prov 8 provides the conceptual and verbal resources that the NT authors used to articulate Christology, without itself being Christology in a strict sense. The passage is a genuine anticipation (type) of what is fulfilled in Christ, not a direct portrait.

title: Wisdom's Seven Pillars: What Do They Represent?
question: Wisdom's Seven Pillars: What Do They Represent?

positions.label: Cosmic completeness: seven = perfect order
positions.argument: Seven is the number of divine completeness in Hebrew literature (creation week, seven-branched lampstand). Wisdom's seven pillars represent her perfect, completely ordered house — the cosmos under wisdom's governance. The image is cosmic: wisdom's house is the ordered universe.

positions.label: Seven wisdom attributes of 1:2–6
positions.argument: The seven pillars correspond to the seven wisdom qualities listed in the prologue (wisdom, instruction, understanding, prudence, righteousness, justice, equity). The architectural image embodies what the prologue names.

positions.label: An unknown architectural reference or literary number
positions.argument: Seven pillars was a specific ancient Near Eastern architectural feature (large public buildings used a seven-pillared hall). The image is architectural, not symbolic, and Wisdom's house is simply a well-built large house.

title: The Retribution Principle in Proverbs: Doctrine or Generalisation?
question: The Retribution Principle in Proverbs: Doctrine or Generalisation?

positions.label: A generally reliable principle, not an absolute guarantee
positions.argument: Proverbs' consistent pairing of righteousness-with-blessing and wickedness-with-consequence is a generally reliable observation about how the ordered world works, not an absolute theological guarantee. The book of Job exists precisely to address cases where the principle appears to fail. Proverbs itself acknowledges exceptions (e.g., the wicked who prosper temporarily).

positions.label: A theological doctrine about God's moral governance
positions.argument: The retribution pairings in Proverbs reflect God's active moral governance of the world — not merely statistical regularity but divine justice. Every antithetical proverb is ultimately a statement about God's character: he rewards the righteous and punishes the wicked. The exceptions are apparent, not real.

positions.label: A idealised picture that Proverbs itself qualifies
positions.argument: Proverbs describes the world as it ought to be and generally is, but with awareness that it is not always so. The book's own qualifications (ch.30's limitations of wisdom; the 'better than' proverbs that qualify simplistic prosperity theology) show the tradition's self-awareness about the principle's limits.

title: Proverbs 11 and the Prosperity Gospel: Does Generosity Guarantee Material Return?
question: Proverbs 11 and the Prosperity Gospel: Does Generosity Guarantee Material Return?

positions.label: A general principle, not a transactional guarantee
positions.argument: The liberality proverbs (11:24–26) describe God's moral order, not a transactional formula. Generally, generous community members receive more communal support; generally, hoarders lose social capital. This is observation, not promise. The prosperity gospel's transactional version ('give and God must give back materially') distorts Proverbs' observation into a manipulative formula.

positions.label: A theological principle of divine economics
positions.argument: The principle is genuinely theological — God's economy works differently from human calculation. The liberality proverbs are making a claim about how God orders material reality, not merely a sociological observation. The principle is real, even if its mechanism is not always visible and its timing is not always immediate.

positions.label: A wisdom idealisation that experience sometimes contradicts
positions.argument: Proverbs states the principle as generally true; experience sometimes contradicts it. Qohelet (Ecclesiastes) and Job both observe that the generous sometimes lose everything while the hoarders sometimes prosper. The principle needs to be held with epistemic humility.

title: Does Proverbs Contain Intimations of Resurrection/Afterlife?
question: Does Proverbs Contain Intimations of Resurrection/Afterlife?

positions.label: Yes: 12:28 and other passages gesture toward eternal life
positions.argument: Proverbs 12:28 ('along that path is no death') and other passages (the tree of life imagery; the contrast between righteous and wicked that extends beyond death) suggest that OT wisdom was developing a theology of life beyond physical death. The NT's resurrection theology has OT roots partly here.

positions.label: No: Proverbs is focused on this-worldly consequences
positions.argument: Proverbs consistently grounds its promises in the observable this-worldly moral order. The 'life' promised is long life in the land; the 'death' threatened is premature physical death and loss of covenant standing. Reading resurrection into Proverbs imports a NT framework onto OT texts.

positions.label: The texts are deliberately ambiguous, pointing toward but not stating a fuller hope
positions.argument: The tree of life imagery, the 12:28 'no death,' and the persistent antithesis between life and death in Proverbs may be more than prudential observations — they may be seeds of the fuller eschatological hope that later biblical theology develops. The ambiguity is itself the point: OT wisdom reaches toward what it cannot yet fully articulate.

title: Does Proverbs 13:24 Endorse Corporal Punishment?
question: Does Proverbs 13:24 Endorse Corporal Punishment?

positions.label: Yes, within appropriate limits
positions.argument: The rod is a real rod, and the proverb is a real endorsement of corporal punishment as a legitimate tool of parental discipline. The multiple rod-passages in Proverbs (13:24; 22:15; 23:13–14; 29:15) form a consistent pattern that cannot be allegorised away. Applied wisely and in love, corporal discipline is the covenant parent's tool.

positions.label: The rod is wisdom's authority, not necessarily a physical instrument
positions.argument: The 'rod' in Proverbs represents the general authority and firmness of discipline, not necessarily a specific physical implement. Ancient shepherds used the rod to guide and correct sheep — the image is of authoritative, engaged, corrective guidance, which may or may not involve physical punishment depending on context.

positions.label: Culturally conditioned; the principle (discipline in love) is universal
positions.argument: The specific cultural practice (corporal punishment in a specific ancient Near Eastern context) is distinct from the universal principle (loving parental discipline is essential to character formation). The principle is non-negotiable; the specific application must be contextualised to contemporary understanding of child development and wellbeing.

title: Proverbs 14:34 and Political Theology: Can Nations Be 'Righteous'?
question: Proverbs 14:34 and Political Theology: Can Nations Be 'Righteous'?

positions.label: Yes: nations are moral agents with covenant obligations
positions.argument: Nations can be and are evaluated by the moral standards of wisdom — not as covenant communities in the OT-Israel sense, but as corporate entities whose policies and cultures reflect righteousness or wickedness. Prov 14:34 applies the personal wisdom ethics to national life directly.

positions.label: The proverb applies to Israel specifically, not all nations
positions.argument: The political theology of this proverb is primarily addressed to Israel as a covenant nation under God's specific governance. Applying it directly to all nations requires the kind of general natural-law extension that is theologically defensible but goes beyond the verse's original context.

positions.label: Both personal and political application is valid
positions.argument: Proverbs 14:34's formulation is universal — 'any people' (leumim), not specifically Israel. The general wisdom-principle that moral faithfulness produces flourishing and wickedness produces reproach is a natural-law observation as much as a covenant promise, applicable to all human communities.

title: The 'Better Than' Proverbs and Prosperity Theology
question: The 'Better Than' Proverbs and Prosperity Theology

positions.label: A direct refutation of material prosperity as the supreme good
positions.argument: The 'better than' proverbs (15:16–17; 16:8, 19) explicitly subordinate material wealth to the fear of the LORD, love, and righteousness. They are one of Proverbs' most direct challenges to any theology that identifies material blessing as the supreme indicator of God's favour.

positions.label: A qualification, not a rejection of prosperity
positions.argument: The 'better than' proverbs qualify the prosperity principle (if you must choose, choose righteousness over wealth) without rejecting the general principle that wisdom leads to material blessing. They are the exception clauses, not the rule.

positions.label: A realistic wisdom about competing goods
positions.argument: The 'better than' proverbs teach the wisdom student to rank competing goods correctly. They do not reject material prosperity as a genuine good — they insist that spiritual and relational goods are more valuable when they conflict with material ones. The ranking exercise is the wisdom pedagogy itself.

title: Divine Sovereignty and Human Responsibility in Proverbs 16
question: Divine Sovereignty and Human Responsibility in Proverbs 16

positions.label: Compatible: both fully true simultaneously
positions.argument: Proverbs 16:1–9 teaches both full human responsibility (the person genuinely plans, genuinely commits, genuinely devises) and full divine sovereignty (the LORD determines the tongue's answer, establishes the plans, directs the steps). These are not competing claims but complementary truths that hold simultaneously without reducing either.

positions.label: Sequential: humans plan; God redirects
positions.argument: Human planning is primary and genuine; God then redirects or adjusts the outcome according to his larger purposes. The sovereignty is supervisory and corrective rather than comprehensive. This reading preserves maximal human responsibility but may understate divine sovereignty.

positions.label: Practical wisdom: plan well, trust God
positions.argument: The proverbs are not making a systematic theological claim about the relationship of divine sovereignty and human agency — they are making the practical wisdom observation that planning and trust belong together. The wisest person commits their work to God and works diligently. The theological resolution is a practical one, not a philosophical one.

title: Love That Covers vs. Enabling: Is Proverbs 17:9 Naively Indulgent?
question: Love That Covers vs. Enabling: Is Proverbs 17:9 Naively Indulgent?

positions.label: Active overlooking in service of relationship, not enabling
positions.argument: Covering an offense is the deliberate choice not to repeatedly surface a wrong that has been addressed or forgiven. It applies to relational offenses between equals, not to abuse or systemic injustice. 1 Pet 4:8 applies this to the covenant community.

positions.label: A principle requiring qualification by accountability texts
positions.argument: The proverb must be read alongside Proverbs' instructions about honest rebuke (27:5–6). Love that covers is not the same as silence that enables harm.

title: 'The Friend Who Sticks Closer Than a Brother' — Christological Reference?
question: 'The Friend Who Sticks Closer Than a Brother' — Christological Reference?

positions.label: A rare but genuinely human friend, primarily
positions.argument: The proverb describes an exceptional human friend whose loyalty exceeds even the primary blood-bond. The application to Christ is a legitimate homiletical extension of the primary meaning.

positions.label: Points toward Christ; the ideal exceeds the human
positions.argument: The friend who exceeds even a brother in closeness points beyond any human relationship to Christ, who never abandons. The ideal described exceeds what any human friend can fully achieve.

title: Proverbs 19:17 and the Theology of Poverty Care
question: Proverbs 19:17 and the Theology of Poverty Care

positions.label: God identifies with the poor: a genuine theological claim
positions.argument: The loan-to-the-LORD metaphor makes an extraordinary claim: giving to the poor is a transaction with God. Matt 25:40 develops this: whatever you did for the least you did for Christ. The theology is genuine, not merely motivational.

positions.label: A practical motivational device, not strict theological identity
positions.argument: The commercial metaphor is primarily persuasive — using the reliability of God's repayment to encourage generosity. The theological claim about divine identity with the poor may read more into the metaphor than it intends.

title: Universal Sinfulness in Proverbs: Does 20:9 Teach a Doctrine of Original Sin?
question: Universal Sinfulness in Proverbs: Does 20:9 Teach a Doctrine of Original Sin?

positions.label: An implicit acknowledgment within the wisdom tradition
positions.argument: Proverbs 20:9 is a rhetorical acknowledgment that no human achieves moral purity — consistent with 1 Kgs 8:46, Eccl 7:20, and Job 25:4. It is a wisdom tradition observation, not a systematic doctrine. The NT develops it into the full doctrine of universal sinfulness.

positions.label: A practical qualification, not a theological doctrine
positions.argument: The verse primarily contextualises the wisdom ideal — it is not claiming a doctrine of original sin but making the practical observation that even the wisdom-oriented person falls short. It is an invitation to humility, not a theological statement about human nature.

title: Divine Sovereignty and Evil Rulers
question: Divine Sovereignty and Evil Rulers

positions.label: Sovereignty is comprehensive
positions.argument: Pharaoh, Nebuchadnezzar, Cyrus — God directs even pagan rulers. Royal moral responsibility coexists with comprehensive divine governance.

positions.label: God permits but does not directly cause evil
positions.argument: Sovereignty operates through permission and governance of consequences rather than direct causation.

title: Proverbs and Amenemope: Dependence or Common Tradition?
question: Proverbs and Amenemope: Dependence or Common Tradition?

positions.label: Proverbs 22-24 adapts Amenemope
positions.argument: The parallels are too specific to be coincidental — structural and verbal similarity indicates literary relationship.

positions.label: Shared ancient Near Eastern wisdom tradition
positions.argument: Shared tradition across cultures is equally plausible and does not require direct literary dependence.

title: Proverbs 23:13-14 and Parental Discipline
question: Proverbs 23:13-14 and Parental Discipline

positions.label: Physical discipline endorsed within appropriate limits
positions.argument: The rod-passages form a consistent pattern. Applied wisely and in love, corporal discipline is a legitimate parental tool.

positions.label: The principle universal; the method contextualised
positions.argument: The principle — firm discipline is an act of love — is non-negotiable. The specific method requires contextualisation to contemporary understanding.

title: Proverbs' Empirical Method: Observation as a Source of Wisdom
question: Proverbs' Empirical Method: Observation as a Source of Wisdom

positions.label: Valid source alongside revelation
positions.argument: The sluggard's-field observation exemplifies the empirical method: observe the world carefully and draw theological conclusions. Observation-based wisdom is a valid route to knowledge.

positions.label: Creation observation as general revelation
positions.argument: Wisdom read from creation expresses natural law embedded within it. God's wisdom is in the structure of things.

title: What Do the Burning Coals Accomplish?
question: What Do the Burning Coals Accomplish?

positions.label: Shame leading to repentance
positions.argument: The discomfort of undeserved kindness produces conscience-burning shame that may lead to changed behaviour.

positions.label: Practical defeat of the enemy
positions.argument: Generous treatment practically disarms the enemy's hostility.

title: The Fool: Type or Continuum?
question: The Fool: Type or Continuum?

positions.label: A distinct character type: the kesil
positions.argument: The kesil has made a settled choice against wisdom's formation. The eleven portraits describe a character, not occasional failures.

positions.label: A continuum from peti to kesil to letz
positions.argument: The three fool-words represent a spectrum of increasing resistance to wisdom.

title: Faithful Wounds: What Makes Rebuke Loving?
question: Faithful Wounds: What Makes Rebuke Loving?

positions.label: Love requires honesty even when it hurts
positions.argument: The wound from a friend is trustworthy because it serves the friend's actual flourishing rather than the comfort of both parties.

positions.label: Rebuke can be weaponised; discernment required
positions.argument: Not all rebuke is loving. The qualification is the rebuker's character and proven friendship.

title: Does Proverbs 28 Teach Prosperity Gospel?
question: Does Proverbs 28 Teach Prosperity Gospel?

positions.label: A general principle, not a transactional formula
positions.argument: The generosity-blessing and greed-punishment patterns describe how the moral order generally works, not guaranteed transactions.

positions.label: A theological principle with genuine economic dimensions
positions.argument: The covenant-blessing and covenant-curse patterns are real theological observations about how God orders material reality.

title: Proverbs 29:18 and Personal Vision: A Misapplication?
question: Proverbs 29:18 and Personal Vision: A Misapplication?

positions.label: About prophetic revelation, not personal goals
positions.argument: Chazon is consistently used for prophetic/divine revelation throughout the OT (Isa 1:1; Ezek 12:22-24; Dan 8:1). The verse addresses the communal need for authoritative divine word, not individual goal-setting.

positions.label: Can be legitimately extended to personal vision
positions.argument: While the primary meaning is prophetic revelation, the principle that purposeful direction from God prevents aimlessness can be legitimately applied to the individual level.

title: Agur's Confession and the Theology of Divine Transcendence
question: Agur's Confession and the Theology of Divine Transcendence

positions.label: Genuine theological humility before divine transcendence
positions.argument: Agur's confession is not false modesty but accurate epistemology: human beings do not have direct access to the divine wisdom that made the cosmos. The questions (who ascended? who cupped the wind?) are genuinely unanswerable from the human side.

positions.label: A rhetorical setup for the canonical affirmation (vv.5-6)
positions.argument: The confession of ignorance sets up the canonical claim that God's words are flawless and sufficient. The point is not perpetual agnosticism but the recognition that what we know, we know because God has revealed it.

title: Is the Eshet Chayil Real Woman, Wisdom Personified, or Both?
question: Is the Eshet Chayil Real Woman, Wisdom Personified, or Both?

positions.label: A real ideal woman, not a personification
positions.argument: The poem is a practical portrait of the covenant household woman -- real, active, embodied. It is an ideal but not an abstract one. The poem is meant to produce praise for real women and to shape the expectations of young men choosing a wife.

positions.label: Lady Wisdom in embodied form
positions.argument: The eshet chayil is Lady Wisdom of chs.1-9 now embodied in a domestic portrait. The poem deliberately echoes the description of Wisdom: she is worth more than rubies (3:15 = 31:10); she speaks with wisdom (31:26 = 8:1-36). The poem closes the Wisdom inclusion that ch.1 opened.

positions.label: Both: a real woman who also personifies covenant wisdom
positions.argument: The poem describes a real type of woman AND serves as the embodiment of the Lady Wisdom figure. Both readings are simultaneously valid and mutually reinforcing.

title: The Historical Value of Matthew's Genealogy
question: Is Matthew's genealogy of Jesus a historical record, a theological construct, or a midrashic composition?
context: Matthew opens his Gospel with a genealogy structured in three groups of 14 generations (Abraham to David, David to exile, exile to Christ). The genealogy omits known kings (Ahaziah, Joash, Amaziah), includes four unexpected women (Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba), and the third section appears to contain only 13 names. These features raise questions: is Matthew working from historical records, constructing a theological argument through selective genealogy, or composing a midrash on Israel's history? The number 14 may be a gematria of David's name (D=4, V=6, D=4).

positions.label: Theological construct, not historical record
positions.proponents: Raymond Brown, John Meier, most historical-critical scholars
positions.argument: Matthew's genealogy contains arithmetical irregularities (the third group has only thirteen names by most counts), includes women not found in the OT genealogies, and is irreconcilable with Luke's genealogy at the post-Davidic level. The three-fourteen structure is clearly artificial. Matthew is constructing a theological argument, not transcribing a registry.
positions.strengths: The deliberate omissions and 3×14 structure demonstrate editorial shaping; the inclusion of the four women serves a theological purpose (gentile and irregular unions in the Messianic line); the third group has only 13 names, suggesting the pattern drives the content.
positions.weaknesses: Theological shaping does not preclude historical foundations; ancient genealogies commonly selected from larger lists; dismissing all historical value may be an overcorrection.

positions.label: Historical foundation with theological shaping
positions.proponents: Richard Bauckham, Craig Keener, N.T. Wright
positions.argument: Genealogical records were kept and consulted in Second Temple Judaism; Josephus mentions his own. Matthew may have had access to Joseph's family records and arranged them theologically. The discrepancy with Luke reflects two different lines of descent (one through Solomon, one through Nathan) and/or two different purposes (legal vs biological). The artificiality of the structure does not require historical invention.
positions.strengths: Matthew likely had access to genealogical records (temple or family archives); the names that can be checked against OT sources are accurate; ancient historians regularly shaped material without fabricating it; the genealogy serves both as historical anchor and theological argument.
positions.weaknesses: The omissions are difficult to explain if the primary intent is historical accuracy; the discrepancies with Luke's genealogy suggest one or both are not strictly historical; the 3×14 structure appears contrived.

positions.label: Midrashic composition
positions.argument: Matthew is composing in the manner of Jewish midrash — using biblical materials and genealogical conventions to make theological points. Historical accuracy in the modern sense is not the genre's concern. What matters is that the theological claims (Davidic descent, Abrahamic fulfilment) are established, not that every name can be verified in an archive.
positions.strengths: Matthew employs recognizable midrashic techniques — gematria (David=14), typological structuring, inclusion of unexpected figures; the genealogy functions as a compressed retelling of Israel's history, not a family tree; this approach was normal and respected in Jewish literary culture.
positions.weaknesses: Calling it 'midrash' may imply fabrication to modern readers, though that is not what the term means in Jewish literary tradition; Matthew's audience may have expected more historical grounding; the midrashic label can be over-applied.

synthesis: Most scholars recognize that Matthew's genealogy is theologically shaped — the 3×14 structure, the omissions, and the inclusion of the four women all serve Matthew's argument that Jesus is the promised Davidic Messiah. This does not necessarily negate historical foundations; ancient genealogies routinely combined real ancestry with purposeful selection and arrangement. The key question is whether Matthew had access to reliable genealogical records or constructed the genealogy from OT texts alone.

title: How Does Matthew 2:23 Fulfil Prophecy?
question: How Does Matthew 2:23 Fulfil Prophecy?

positions.label: Collective reference to the prophetic pattern
positions.argument: No single OT text says 'he shall be called a Nazarene.' Matthew refers to the prophetic pattern of the despised servant.

positions.label: Reference to Isa 11:1 specifically
positions.argument: The Hebrew netser (branch) in Isa 11:1 is the closest single-text connection to Nazareth.

title: Was John's Baptism Christian Baptism?
question: Was John's Baptism Christian Baptism?

positions.label: John's baptism was preparatory; Christian baptism is distinct
positions.argument: John baptized in water for repentance; Christian baptism is in the name of the Trinity and linked to the Spirit's work (Acts 2:38; 19:1-6).

positions.label: Continuity of covenant sign
positions.argument: Both point to cleansing and covenant membership; the difference is degree of fulfilment, not essential nature.

title: The Temptation: Real or Staged?
question: The Temptation: Real or Staged?

positions.label: Genuinely real — Jesus could have yielded
positions.argument: If not genuinely possible to accept, it was not a real temptation. The fully human Jesus was genuinely tempted; the fully divine Son was incapable of sinning. The tension is the Christological mystery (Heb 4:15).

positions.label: Representative trial — Jesus as Israel's covenant head
positions.argument: Jesus as the true Israel faces and defeats the tests Israel failed in the wilderness. The typological significance gives the event its redemptive-historical meaning.

title: The Antitheses: Contradiction or Deepening of Torah?
question: The Antitheses: Contradiction or Deepening of Torah?

positions.label: Deepening and completing — not contradicting
positions.argument: Jesus brings out the fullest meaning of the OT. The six antitheses uncover internal demands that external commands always implied.

positions.label: Some antitheses represent genuine modification
positions.argument: The divorce and oath antitheses represent genuine modifications of Mosaic permission (Deut 24:1-4; Num 30) rather than mere deepening.

title: The Lord's Prayer: Model or Formula?
question: The Lord's Prayer: Model or Formula?

positions.label: A model for the structure and content of prayer
positions.argument: Pray like this indicates a model, not a verbatim formula. The prayer teaches priorities, posture, and petitions appropriate to kingdom citizens.

positions.label: A specific formula to be repeated
positions.argument: Pray like this combined with Luke's parallel ('when you pray, say') supports regular verbatim use as a liturgical prayer.

title: Matthew 7:21-23: Can Genuine Christians Be Rejected?
question: Matthew 7:21-23: Can Genuine Christians Be Rejected?

positions.label: These are counterfeit believers, never genuinely saved
positions.argument: The rejection 'I never knew you' (ouk egnon -- never at any point) indicates no genuine relationship ever existed.

positions.label: These are genuine believers who were unfaithful
positions.argument: The 'Lord, Lord' and works performed suggest genuine confession and service. The rejection is due to persistent unfaithfulness.

title: Does Matthew 8:17 Teach Physical Healing in the Atonement?
question: Does Matthew 8:17 Teach Physical Healing in the Atonement?

positions.label: Yes -- Isaiah 53:4 applies to physical healing
positions.proponents: Word of Faith theology, some Pentecostals
positions.argument: Matthew's application of Isa 53:4 to physical healing establishes it as part of the atonement's provision.

positions.label: No -- Matthew describes the ministry, not the atonement mechanism
positions.argument: Matthew applies Isa 53:4 to the healing ministry as its own fulfilment. The atonement (Isa 53:5) addresses sin; the healing ministry (53:4) demonstrates compassionate bearing of human suffering.

title: Matthew 9:18 vs. Mark 5:23 -- Was Jairus's Daughter Already Dead?
question: Matthew 9:18 vs. Mark 5:23 -- Was Jairus's Daughter Already Dead?

positions.label: Matthew: already dead; Mark: dying but not yet dead
positions.argument: Matthew reports the daughter as 'just now dead' when Jairus arrives; Mark has her still alive at the time of the request, dying during Jesus' travel.

positions.label: Matthean compression of the narrative
positions.argument: Matthew regularly condenses narratives for theological purposes. Jairus may have reported her as practically dead. Matthew omits the messengers' arrival preserved in Mark 5:35.

title: Matthew 10:23 -- 'You Will Not Finish Going Through the Cities of Israel Before the Son of Man Comes'
question: What did Jesus mean by 'you will not finish going through the cities of Israel before the Son of Man comes'?
context: In Matthew 10:23, during the mission discourse, Jesus tells the Twelve they will not finish going through Israel's cities 'before the Son of Man comes.' This saying is notoriously difficult. If it refers to the Second Coming, Jesus appears to have been wrong — the disciples completed their mission and Christ has not returned. If it refers to AD 70, it requires 'the coming of the Son of Man' to mean something other than the Parousia. If it refers only to the immediate mission, 'the Son of Man comes' needs a non-eschatological referent. The verse is a crux for any theory of Jesus' eschatological expectations.

positions.label: Fulfilled in AD 70 with the destruction of Jerusalem
positions.argument: The coming of the Son of Man refers to the judgment on Jerusalem in AD 70, not the Second Coming.
positions.strengths: The destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 was a 'coming' of the Son of Man in judgment (cf. Dan 7:13 as vindication/enthronement); Matthew 24 closely parallels this saying and is widely interpreted as referring to AD 70; avoids the failed-prophecy problem.
positions.weaknesses: 'The coming of the Son of Man' is typically associated with the Parousia in Matthew (24:30, 25:31); the AD 70 reading requires redefining a well-known eschatological phrase; the mission to Israel continued well past AD 70.

positions.label: Refers to the Second Coming, establishing the urgency of the ongoing mission
positions.argument: The verse establishes the continuing urgency of the Jewish mission until the end of history.
positions.strengths: Preserves the natural meaning of 'the Son of Man comes'; establishes the urgency of mission — there is always more to do before Christ returns; the ongoing incompleteness of Jewish evangelism gives the saying perennial relevance.
positions.weaknesses: Seems to predict Christ's return within the disciples' lifetime, which did not occur; the 'delay of the Parousia' is a well-documented early church problem (2 Pet 3:3-4); risks making Jesus a false prophet.

positions.label: Limited to the immediate context of the disciples' mission
positions.argument: The verse applies specifically to the first missionary journey -- they will not finish visiting all Israel before Jesus himself arrives to advance the mission.
positions.strengths: The mission discourse addresses the Twelve's immediate journey; 'the Son of Man comes' may refer to Jesus catching up with the disciples or to the end of this specific mission phase; the simplest contextual reading.
positions.weaknesses: 'The coming of the Son of Man' is far too weighty a phrase for a simple travel itinerary; no other NT passage uses this language for a non-eschatological event; trivializes the saying.

synthesis: No interpretation is free of difficulty. The AD 70 reading has gained ground since the recognition that 'the coming of the Son of Man' in Daniel 7:13 describes enthronement before God, not necessarily descent to earth. The Parousia reading preserves eschatological urgency but raises the 'delay of the Parousia' problem. The immediate-mission reading avoids both issues but gives 'the Son of Man comes' an unusually weak referent. Most scholars treat this as a deliberately multivalent saying that addresses both the immediate mission and the eschatological horizon.

title: Matthew 11:28-30 -- Does 'Rest' Mean Salvation or Discipleship?
question: Matthew 11:28-30 -- Does 'Rest' Mean Salvation or Discipleship?

positions.label: Rest as salvation -- the relief of forgiveness
positions.argument: The weary and burdened are those exhausted by sin and law; the rest is the relief of forgiveness and acceptance. The gospel invitation is primarily soteriological.

positions.label: Rest as discipleship -- the peace of learning from Christ
positions.argument: Take my yoke and learn from me describes ongoing discipleship. The rest is the settled peace of a life governed by a gentle master rather than a harsh taskmaster.

title: What Is the Blasphemy Against the Holy Spirit?
question: What Is the Blasphemy Against the Holy Spirit?

positions.label: Attributing the Spirit works to Satan after full exposure
positions.argument: The specific context: the Pharisees have seen undeniable Spirit-works and chosen to call them demonic. This wilful inversion after full knowledge is the sin.

positions.label: Final, persistent unrepentance
positions.argument: The unforgivable sin is not a single act but the settled disposition of total resistance to grace that prevents repentance throughout one's life.

title: Why Did Jesus Teach in Parables? Revelation or Concealment?
question: Why Did Jesus Teach in Parables? Revelation or Concealment?

positions.label: Revelation -- to make truth accessible
positions.argument: Parables make abstract truth concrete and memorable. They are pedagogical tools for illumination.

positions.label: Both revelation and concealment -- depending on the hearer
positions.argument: Matthew 13:11-17 explicitly states that parables reveal to disciples and conceal from those outside. The Isaianic citation (Isa 6:9-10) makes the concealing function intentional.

title: Was the Feeding of the Five Thousand Eucharistic?
question: Was the Feeding of the Five Thousand Eucharistic?

positions.label: Yes -- deliberate foreshadowing
positions.proponents: Most patristic and many modern commentators
positions.argument: The fourfold action (took, blessed, broke, gave) exactly mirrors the institution of the Eucharist (26:26).

positions.label: No -- provision only
positions.argument: The eucharistic reading imports later theology. The miracle is about compassionate physical provision.

title: The Canaanite Woman: Is Jesus Harsh or Testing?
question: The Canaanite Woman: Is Jesus Harsh or Testing?

positions.label: Testing her faith
positions.argument: The apparent harshness is pedagogical: Jesus draws out her faith by three apparent repulsions. The healing immediately follows her creative persistence.

positions.label: A real limitation of the first-phase mission
positions.argument: The restriction to Israel is historically real. The exception is genuine and signals the coming expansion.

title: What Is the Rock in Matthew 16:18?
question: When Jesus says 'on this rock I will build my church,' does 'the rock' refer to Peter, his confession, or Christ himself?
context: Matthew 16:18 — 'You are Peter (Petros), and on this rock (petra) I will build my church' — is arguably the most ecclesiologically consequential verse in the NT. Roman Catholic tradition identifies the rock as Peter and his apostolic office, grounding papal authority. Protestant tradition has generally identified the rock as Peter's confession ('You are the Christ') or as Christ himself. The Greek word play (Petros/petra) and the Aramaic substratum (Kepha for both) are central to the debate.

positions.label: Peter himself
positions.argument: Peter is the rock; his successors (popes) inherit his authority. The Aramaic background (kepha) supports identifying Peter directly.
positions.strengths: The Aramaic Kepha is used for both 'Peter' and 'rock,' making the identification natural; the immediate context is about Peter — he is named, blessed, and given the keys; the early church fathers (Origen, Cyprian) often identify Peter as the rock; Ephesians 2:20 says the church is built on 'apostles and prophets.'
positions.weaknesses: Peter's role does not automatically establish papal succession; Peter himself errs immediately after (16:22-23 — 'Get behind me, Satan'); the NT presents Peter as first among equals, not as a monarchical authority; 1 Corinthians 3:11 says Christ is the only foundation.

positions.label: Peter's confession of Christ
positions.proponents: Most Protestant and Eastern Orthodox interpreters
positions.argument: The church is built on the truth Peter confessed. Peter is the representative confessor, not the exclusive foundation.
positions.strengths: The petra ('rock') may refer to the confessional content ('You are the Christ') rather than to Peter personally; the church is built on christological confession, not on a fallible individual; this reading is dominant in Reformed tradition and avoids papalist implications; the shift from Petros to petra in Greek may signal a distinction.
positions.weaknesses: The Aramaic Kepha-Kepha wordplay makes a distinction between Peter and the rock unlikely in the original language; the immediate context is personal — Jesus addresses Peter by name; this reading may be motivated more by anti-papal polemic than by exegesis.

positions.label: Christ himself
positions.argument: 1 Cor 3:11: no one can lay any foundation other than Jesus Christ.
positions.strengths: Paul explicitly says 'no one can lay any foundation other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ' (1 Cor 3:11); 1 Peter 2:4-8 identifies Christ as the cornerstone and living stone; Isaiah 28:16 and Psalm 118:22 use 'rock/stone' for God/Messiah, not for humans.
positions.weaknesses: The immediate context is about Peter, not Christ; Jesus says 'you are Peter, and on this rock' — the demonstrative 'this' points to Peter, not to a third party; reading Christ as the rock requires ignoring the wordplay entirely.

synthesis: The Aramaic behind the Greek almost certainly used Kepha ('rock') for both — making the wordplay a clear identification of Peter as the rock. However, even many Catholic scholars acknowledge that 'the rock' is Peter-confessing-Christ, not Peter as an isolated individual. Most Protestants now accept that Peter is the rock in some sense (the foundational apostolic witness) while denying that this establishes a perpetual papal office. The key exegetical question has shifted from 'Is Peter the rock?' (probably yes) to 'Does this establish papal succession?' (contested).

title: What Caused the Disciples' Failure? (17:19-21)
question: What Caused the Disciples' Failure? (17:19-21)

positions.label: Insufficient faith
positions.argument: The disciples had faith but not the quality of undivided, dependent faith that this exorcism required. The mustard seed saying is about the purity of faith, not its quantity.

positions.label: Lack of prayer and fasting (v.21)
positions.argument: The failure was a failure of spiritual preparation -- prayer and fasting are the disciplines that develop the faith capable of this exorcism.

title: Does Matthew 18:15-20 Describe Church Discipline or Conflict Resolution?
question: Does Matthew 18:15-20 Describe Church Discipline or Conflict Resolution?

positions.label: Formal church discipline
positions.argument: The three-stage procedure, binding-and-loosing authority, and the final 'treat as a pagan' decision make this a formal church governance procedure.

positions.label: Interpersonal conflict resolution
positions.argument: The context (Peter's forgiveness question immediately follows) and the goal of 'winning your brother' suggest the primary concern is relational restoration, with formal discipline as the last resort.

title: What Does the Exception Clause (porneia) Permit?
question: Does the 'exception clause' in Matthew 5:32 and 19:9 permit divorce and remarriage, divorce only, or does it refer solely to betrothal unfaithfulness?
context: Matthew's version of Jesus' teaching on divorce includes the phrase 'except for sexual immorality' (porneia) — absent from Mark 10:11-12 and Luke 16:18. This 'exception clause' (also called the Matthean exception) has generated enormous debate. Does porneia permit both divorce and remarriage? Does it permit separation without the right to remarry? Or does it refer only to sexual unfaithfulness discovered during the betrothal period (as in Joseph's situation with Mary, Matt 1:19)? The practical and pastoral stakes are enormous.

positions.label: Divorce and remarriage permitted for sexual immorality
positions.argument: Porneia = sexual immorality; the innocent party may divorce and remarry. This is the standard evangelical position, consistent with the plain reading of the Greek.
positions.strengths: The most natural reading of the exception clause — 'except for porneia' creates a genuine exception to the prohibition; aligns with Deuteronomy 24:1-4 which permitted divorce and assumed remarriage; 1 Corinthians 7:15 may add abandonment as a second ground; dominant Protestant reading.
positions.weaknesses: Mark and Luke contain no exception clause — is Matthew adding to Jesus' teaching? The early church almost unanimously opposed remarriage after divorce; a too-broad definition of porneia can become a loophole; Jesus' teaching in context is restrictive, not permissive.

positions.label: Divorce permitted but not remarriage
positions.proponents: Many early church fathers, some Catholic scholars
positions.argument: The exception permits divorce (separation) but remarriage is not explicitly authorized. The 'no adultery' clause in v.9b applies to the divorced person who remarries.
positions.strengths: The early church fathers (Hermas, Origen, Augustine) permitted separation but not remarriage; marriage is a permanent covenant ('what God has joined together,' 19:6); the exception clause permits divorce (separation) but 'whoever marries a divorced woman commits adultery' (5:32b) prohibits remarriage; dominant Catholic and Orthodox reading.
positions.weaknesses: In Jewish law, divorce always implied the right to remarry — a 'divorce without remarriage' would have been conceptually foreign; the Greek apolysē ('divorce') carries legal force implying full dissolution; may impose an unrealistic burden on the innocent party.

positions.label: Betrothal unchastity only
positions.argument: Porneia refers to unchastity discovered during the betrothal period (Joseph's situation in 1:18-19). After consummation, there is no exception.
positions.strengths: Porneia may refer specifically to sexual infidelity during the betrothal period (cf. Matt 1:18-19 where Joseph considers 'divorcing' Mary); explains why only Matthew includes the exception — his Jewish audience understood betrothal law; preserves the absolute prohibition on divorce within consummated marriage.
positions.weaknesses: Porneia has a broad semantic range in the NT (fornication, sexual immorality generally) and is not restricted to betrothal; this reading makes the exception clause irrelevant to most married Christians; betrothal practices were fading by the time Matthew wrote.

synthesis: The divorce-and-remarriage reading is dominant in Protestant tradition and reflects the majority evangelical position. The no-remarriage view (early church, Catholic tradition) treats marriage as ontologically indissoluble. The betrothal view is a minority position but explains why only Matthew includes the exception — his Jewish audience understood betrothal infidelity as a distinct category. No consensus exists, and pastoral practice varies widely even within denominations that share the same theological position.

title: Are the 'Many' in the Ransom Saying Limited?
question: Are the 'Many' in the Ransom Saying Limited?

positions.label: Many = all humanity (universal scope)
positions.argument: The OT background (Isa 53:11-12) uses 'many' to mean 'the great multitude' -- the all-encompassing extent of the Servant's work. In context it means all without restriction.

positions.label: Many = the elect (limited scope)
positions.argument: The many are those for whom the ransom is applied effectively -- the elect. The 'ransom for many' is distinguished from 'for all' to preserve the particularity of redemption.

title: Was the Temple Cleansing a Political or Religious Act?
question: Was the Temple Cleansing a Political or Religious Act?

positions.label: Primarily religious: restoration of prayer-house purpose
positions.argument: Jesus's OT citations (Isa 56:7; Jer 7:11) define the issue as worship vs. commerce. The cleansing restores the Temple to its designated function.

positions.label: Politically motivated: a challenge to priestly authority
positions.argument: The Temple economy was controlled by the high-priestly families. Jesus's action challenged their economic and religious power -- triggering the plot to kill him.

title: What Is the Wedding Garment in Matthew 22:11-12?
question: What Is the Wedding Garment in Matthew 22:11-12?

positions.label: Righteousness imputed at justification
positions.argument: The garment is the righteousness of Christ credited to the believer. The man without the garment is present in the visible church but has not received the righteousness that genuine faith receives.

positions.label: Holy living and transformation
positions.argument: The garment is the moral transformation that genuine reception of the gospel produces. The man represents the nominal Christian who has joined without being changed.

title: To Whom Are the Woes Addressed? Are They Applicable to the Church?
question: To Whom Are the Woes Addressed? Are They Applicable to the Church?

positions.label: Specifically to first-century scribes and Pharisees
positions.argument: The woes address a specific religious culture of the first century. Broader application requires careful translation.

positions.label: To all who exercise religious authority hypocritically
positions.argument: The patterns described -- performing for audience, tithing to avoid ethics, building tombs for murdered prophets -- are perennial religious temptations the church in every generation must guard against.

title: Does the Olivet Discourse Refer to AD 70, the Future Parousia, or Both?
question: Does the Olivet Discourse Refer to AD 70, the Future Parousia, or Both?

positions.label: Entirely AD 70 (preterist)
positions.argument: All of vv.4-34 was fulfilled in AD 70. V.36 begins the discussion of the actual end-time parousia.

positions.label: Entirely future (dispensationalist)
positions.argument: The discourse is entirely future-oriented. The Abomination of Desolation is still future. 'This generation' means the generation alive at the end.

positions.label: Double reference: AD 70 + future parousia
positions.argument: The discourse uses the near event (AD 70) as a type and first-stage fulfilment of the far event (the parousia). Vv.4-34 have both a near and a far referent; v.36 shifts exclusively to the end-time.

title: Who Are 'the Least of These' in Matthew 25:40?
question: Who Are 'the Least of These' in Matthew 25:40?

positions.label: All the poor universally
positions.proponents: Patristic consensus (especially Chrysostom), most modern readers
positions.argument: Christ is present in all suffering humanity. The judgment is on universal charity.

positions.label: Specifically Jesus's disciples/missionaries
positions.argument: Matthew's use of 'brothers' consistently refers to disciples. The vulnerable missionaries sent out in ch.10 are 'the least of these.'

positions.label: Both, in concentric circles
positions.argument: The immediate reference is to disciples; the principle extends to all the vulnerable because the disciples represent them.

title: Real Presence or Memorial in the Lord's Supper?
question: Is Christ bodily present in the Lord's Supper, spiritually present, or is it purely a memorial?
context: Jesus' words at the Last Supper — 'This is my body' (Matt 26:26) — have generated the deepest and most consequential division in Christian history. The four major interpretations (transubstantiation, consubstantiation, spiritual presence, memorial) each claim strong exegetical and theological support. The debate shaped the Reformation and continues to divide Catholic, Lutheran, Reformed, and Baptist traditions. The question is not merely academic — it determines whether the Eucharist is a sacrifice, a sacrament, or an ordinance.

positions.label: Transubstantiation: substance becomes body and blood
positions.argument: This is my body is a literal identity statement; the substance of the elements is changed.
positions.strengths: Takes 'this is my body' with maximum seriousness; supported by the early church fathers (Ignatius, Cyril, Ambrose); John 6:51-58 ('unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man') seems to require physical eating; the dominant view for the first 1,500 years of Christianity.
positions.weaknesses: Relies on Aristotelian substance/accidents distinction foreign to the biblical text; the institution narrative uses symbolic language throughout ('this cup is the new covenant'); 'is' (estin) can mean 'represents' in Greek and Aramaic.

positions.label: Consubstantiation: body and blood present in, with, under the elements
positions.argument: The body and blood are truly present alongside the bread and wine.
positions.strengths: Takes 'this is my body' literally without requiring Aristotelian metaphysics; Christ is truly present in, with, and under the bread — no transformation occurs; Luther's analogy: as iron glows with fire but remains iron, the bread bears Christ's body while remaining bread.
positions.weaknesses: The 'in, with, and under' formula has no direct biblical basis; the ubiquity of Christ's body (required for this view) is theologically debated; Scripture does not explain the mechanism of presence.

positions.label: Spiritual presence received by faith
positions.argument: Christ is truly present but spiritually -- received by faith. The bread and wine are signs and pledges.
positions.strengths: Affirms real presence without physical transformation; Christ is received spiritually by faith through the Holy Spirit; Calvin's view mediates between Luther's physicalism and Zwingli's bare memorialism; honors the sursum corda ('lift up your hearts') tradition — believers are lifted to Christ, not Christ brought down.
positions.weaknesses: The mechanism of 'spiritual presence' is difficult to define precisely; 'this is my body' seems stronger than a merely spiritual reading; the Reformed position can appear as an unstable compromise.

positions.label: Memorial only
positions.argument: This is my body means this represents my body. The Supper is a commemoration.
positions.strengths: 'Do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19; 1 Cor 11:24-25) defines the meal's primary purpose; 'this is my body' is clearly figurative — Jesus was physically present when he said it, making literal identification impossible; avoids all metaphysical speculation; the simplest reading.
positions.weaknesses: May underestimate the force of 'this is my body'; Paul's warning about eating 'unworthily' (1 Cor 11:27-29) implies something more than mere remembrance; strips the meal of sacramental power; historically a minority view until the 16th century.

synthesis: Each tradition has developed sophisticated theological arguments. Roman Catholic transubstantiation rests on Aristotelian metaphysics and patristic tradition. Lutheran consubstantiation takes 'This is my body' as literally as possible without transubstantiation. Calvin's spiritual presence mediates between Luther and Zwingli. The memorial view appeals to 'do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19). Ecumenical dialogue has narrowed some gaps, but fundamental differences about the nature of Christ's presence remain the primary barrier to Christian unity.

title: What Happened to the Saints Raised at the Crucifixion? (27:52-53)
question: What Happened to the Saints Raised at the Crucifixion? (27:52-53)

positions.label: Literal bodily resurrection, appearing after Easter

positions.label: Symbolic/apocalyptic sign
positions.argument: The event is Matthew's apocalyptic narration of the cross's cosmic significance -- the language is symbolic rather than describing a literal event.
