positions.argument: The raising is real but its appearance is the Matthean way of connecting the cross to the resurrection as a unified redemptive event.

title: Was the Resurrected Christ's Body Physical or Spiritual?
question: Was the Resurrected Christ's Body Physical or Spiritual?

positions.label: Physical and recognisable, though transformed
positions.argument: The women take hold of his feet (v.9); the disciples worship him. The resurrection body is the transformed version of the crucified body -- continuous but glorified, not a ghost.

positions.label: Primarily spiritual/pneumatic
positions.argument: The resurrection appearances are spiritual experiences of the disciples' faith in the living Christ, not physical encounters with a resurrected body.

title: Was Mark written before or after AD 70?
question: Was the Gospel of Mark written before the destruction of the temple in AD 70, or after it?
context: The dating of Mark's Gospel is crucial for understanding its Olivet Discourse (Mark 13) and the development of early Christianity. Pre-70 dating (c. 65-68) places Mark during Nero's persecution and before the temple's destruction, meaning Mark 13 is genuine prophecy. Post-70 dating (c. 70-75) allows Mark 13 to be shaped by knowledge of the temple's fall, meaning the discourse reflects vaticinium ex eventu (prophecy after the event). The debate affects how we read Mark's eschatology and his reliability as a historical source.

positions.label: Before AD 70
positions.proponents: Papias, Irenaeus; most conservative scholars. Mark 13 predicts the Temple’s destruction; if written after 70, fulfilment would be noted more explicitly. Composition c.AD 50–65.
positions.argument: Pre-70 date
positions.strengths: Mark 13's predictions do not closely match the actual events of AD 70 (no mention of fire, Titus, or the specific circumstances); early church tradition (Irenaeus, Clement of Alexandria) associates Mark with Peter in Rome, likely during Nero's persecution (c. 64-68); the abrupt ending at 16:8 suggests urgency consistent with a crisis period.
positions.weaknesses: Mark 13:14 ('the abomination that causes desolation standing where it does not belong') could reflect knowledge of the temple's desecration; the urgency of the discourse may indicate a recent catastrophe; dating based on internal evidence is inherently uncertain.

positions.label: After AD 70
positions.proponents: Some critical scholars. The specificity of Mark 13 and its use as a source by Matthew and Luke (both post-70) may indicate Mark itself is c.AD 70–75.
positions.argument: Post-70 date
positions.strengths: Mark 13's warnings to flee Judea and the detailed apocalyptic language may reflect awareness of the Jewish War (AD 66-70); the discourse's structure (temple prediction → persecution → cosmic signs → Son of Man) could be shaped by recent events; most critical scholars favor a date around AD 70.
positions.weaknesses: The prophecy's details do not closely match the actual destruction — genuine vaticinium ex eventu would be more specific; Mark contains no clear 'post-destruction' theological reflection (unlike Matthew and Luke); the argument risks circular reasoning — dating the text by its prophecy and then judging the prophecy by the dating.

synthesis: The majority scholarly view places Mark around AD 65-75, with the precise date hinging on whether Mark 13 presupposes knowledge of the temple's destruction. Pre-70 scholars note that Mark 13's details do not closely match what actually happened in AD 70 (no mention of fire, for instance), suggesting genuine prediction. Post-70 scholars argue the discourse's urgency and detail reflect awareness of recent events. The truth may be that Mark was written close enough to AD 70 that the question cannot be definitively resolved.

title: Did Jesus abolish the Sabbath?
question: Did Jesus abolish the Sabbath, or did he reinterpret it while affirming its ongoing validity?
context: Mark records multiple Sabbath controversies (2:23-28; 3:1-6) in which Jesus challenges Pharisaic Sabbath restrictions. His declaration that 'the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath' (2:27) and 'the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath' (2:28) have been read either as abolishing the Sabbath or as reclaiming its original purpose. The question has practical implications for whether Christians are obligated to observe a Sabbath day and for the relationship between the Old and New Covenants.

positions.label: Abolition view
positions.proponents: Some Reformation and modern interpreters read 2:27-28 as annulling the Sabbath command entirely for Christians.
positions.argument: Abolition
positions.strengths: Colossians 2:16-17 warns against judging others regarding 'a Sabbath day' — the Sabbath was a 'shadow' whose reality is found in Christ; Romans 14:5 relativizes sacred days; the early church shifted worship to Sunday (the Lord's Day); the new covenant supersedes Sabbath obligation.
positions.weaknesses: Jesus never explicitly abolishes the Sabbath in Mark — he challenges misinterpretation, not the institution; 'Lord of the Sabbath' implies authority over it, not negation of it; Jesus regularly observed the Sabbath; the abolition reading may reflect later Pauline theology projected back onto the Synoptics.

positions.label: Transformation view
positions.proponents: Jesus restores the Sabbath’s original purpose rather than abolishing it. The Son of Man as Sabbath Lord re-establishes the Sabbath under new covenant terms.
positions.argument: Transformation
positions.strengths: Jesus reclaims the Sabbath's original humanitarian purpose ('made for man'); he does not abolish it but reinterprets it around himself — he is Lord of the Sabbath; the Sabbath is transformed, not eliminated; 'the Sabbath was made for man' presupposes its ongoing value; healing on the Sabbath shows what the day is for.
positions.weaknesses: If the Sabbath is merely transformed, Christians should still observe a weekly day of rest — which many do not; the boundary between 'transformation' and 'abolition' can be unclear in practice; some argue this reading keeps Christians under Torah obligation.

synthesis: Jesus did not abolish the Sabbath in Mark's narrative — he observed it (attending synagogue, Luke 4:16) while challenging human additions to it. His claim to be 'Lord of the Sabbath' asserts authority over the institution, not its elimination. Whether the Sabbath continues for Christians is a separate question answered differently across traditions. Most scholars agree that Jesus' Sabbath conflicts are about authority and interpretation, not about abolition.

title: What is the unforgivable sin?
question: What is the 'blasphemy against the Holy Spirit' that will never be forgiven?
context: In Mark 3:28-30, Jesus declares that 'whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will never be forgiven; they are guilty of an eternal sin.' Mark explains: 'He said this because they were saying, "He has an impure spirit"' (3:30). The scribes attributed Jesus' exorcisms — works of the Holy Spirit — to Beelzebul. This passage has caused enormous pastoral anxiety: countless Christians have feared they have committed the unforgivable sin. The two main interpretive traditions differ on whether the sin is a specific historical act or a persistent spiritual state.

positions.label: Specific blasphemy view
positions.proponents: The sin is unique to those who witnessed Jesus’s exorcisms and attributed them to Satan — a historically bounded sin not available to later generations.
positions.argument: Historically bounded
positions.strengths: Mark 3:30 explicitly identifies the occasion — attributing the Spirit's work to Satan; this is a specific, observable, historical act; the sin requires full knowledge (the scribes knew Jesus' works were genuine) combined with willful rejection; limits the sin to a defined category rather than creating existential anxiety.
positions.weaknesses: If the sin is only the specific historical act of the scribes, it may no longer be committable today; does not address the broader pattern of persistent rejection that Hebrews 6 and 10 describe; may seem to restrict the warning to a past context.

positions.label: Persistent state view
positions.proponents: The sin is a permanent hardening against the Spirit’s testimony that can occur in any generation, whenever someone persistently and finally rejects the clear evidence of the Spirit’s work.
positions.argument: Persistent hardening
positions.strengths: The scribes' response represents a pattern — sustained, deliberate rejection of the Spirit's testimony to Christ; Hebrews 6:4-6 and 10:26-29 describe a similar state of irrevocable apostasy; the sin is not a single act but a settled disposition of heart; explains why it is unforgivable — not because God's grace is insufficient, but because the person has permanently closed themselves to it.
positions.weaknesses: May create pastoral anxiety — how does one know if their rejection is 'persistent enough'? The boundary between serious doubt and permanent apostasy is unclear; may seem to limit God's grace.

synthesis: Most scholars agree that the 'specific blasphemy' and 'persistent state' views are not mutually exclusive. The historical occasion was the scribes' deliberate attribution of the Spirit's work to Satan — a calculated, informed rejection, not an impulsive mistake. The very fact that someone is anxious about having committed this sin is strong evidence they have not — the unforgivable sin involves a hardened, deliberate, and sustained rejection of the Spirit's testimony to Christ. Jesus' point is not to terrify the faithful but to warn the willfully blind.

title: Is Mary's consent to the Annunciation essential to the Incarnation?
question: Was Mary's 'Let it be to me' a necessary condition for the Incarnation, or was it a faithful response to a divine fait accompli?
context: When the angel Gabriel announces that Mary will conceive the Son of God, Mary responds: 'I am the Lord's servant. Let it be to me according to your word' (Luke 1:38). Catholic and Orthodox theology has given this response enormous significance — Mary's fiat is her free consent to cooperate with God's saving plan, making her the 'New Eve' whose obedience reverses Eve's disobedience. Protestant theology generally views Mary's response as faithful acceptance but not as a necessary condition — God's sovereign plan would have proceeded regardless.

positions.label: Necessary consent view
positions.proponents: Mary’s "may your word to me be fulfilled" (1:38) is a genuine consent without which the Incarnation would not proceed. The Incarnation required a human yes.
positions.argument: Consent as condition
positions.strengths: God consistently works through free human response throughout Scripture (Abraham's faith, Moses' obedience); Mary's fiat is the hinge of salvation history — the New Eve's obedience answering the Old Eve's disobedience (Irenaeus, Justin); God respects human freedom even in the most decisive moment of history; the annunciation narrative is structured as a dialogue, not a decree.
positions.weaknesses: Makes the Incarnation contingent on a human decision — what if Mary had said no? God's sovereign plan for salvation cannot depend on a creature's consent; Protestant theology insists that grace is effectual, not merely offered; the concept of 'necessary consent' risks elevating Mary above her role.

positions.label: Divine sovereignty view
positions.proponents: God’s election of Mary was unconditional; her consent is the gracious response to what God was already doing, not a condition of its occurrence.
positions.argument: Election precedes consent
positions.strengths: God's eternal plan of salvation is not contingent on any human decision (Eph 1:4-5; Gal 4:4); Mary's response is faithful but not causative — God would have accomplished the Incarnation regardless; the sovereignty of God ensures that his purposes cannot be frustrated; Mary is a model of obedient faith, not a co-agent of redemption.
positions.weaknesses: May reduce Mary to a passive vessel rather than a willing participant; the annunciation narrative structures the encounter as a genuine dialogue with a genuine response; if consent is irrelevant, the narrative loses dramatic and theological weight; risks making God coercive.

synthesis: The theological divide reflects broader differences about the relationship between divine sovereignty and human cooperation. The Catholic emphasis on Mary's consent reflects a synergistic theology of grace — God works through free human response. The Protestant emphasis on divine sovereignty reflects a monergistic theology — God's plan does not depend on human consent. Both affirm Mary as a model of faith; they differ on whether her consent was constitutive of the Incarnation or responsive to it.

title: Was the census historical?
question: Did the census described in Luke 2:1-3 actually occur, or is it a Lukan theological construction?
context: Luke 2:1-3 states that Caesar Augustus ordered a census of 'the entire Roman world' and that this was 'the first census taken while Quirinius was governing Syria.' The historical problems are significant: Josephus dates Quirinius' census to AD 6/7, but Jesus' birth under Herod the Great (died 4 BC) would predate this by a decade. No Roman source records an empire-wide census under Augustus. The requirement to travel to an ancestral town has no known Roman parallel. These difficulties have led some scholars to question the historicity of Luke's account.

positions.label: Historical view
positions.proponents: Luke’s census fits within the Augustan census programme (documented by Josephus and papyri). An earlier Quirinius census or a different official is possible.
positions.argument: Historical
positions.strengths: Luke is a careful historian (Luke 1:1-4; his accuracy in Acts is repeatedly confirmed by archaeology); Quirinius may have served in Syria twice — an earlier military role and the later census role; Roman provincial census administration is poorly documented for this period; Egyptian papyri confirm 14-year census cycles.
positions.weaknesses: No evidence for a double tenure by Quirinius survives; no Roman source records an empire-wide census under Augustus; the return-to-ancestral-town requirement is unparalleled in Roman practice; the chronological gap between Herod's death (4 BC) and Quirinius' known census (AD 6/7) is substantial.

positions.label: Anachronistic view
positions.proponents: Luke may have confused a later AD 6 Quirinius census with Herod’s reign. The historical difficulty is acknowledged even among conservative scholars.
positions.argument: Possible error in attribution
positions.strengths: The historical problems are real — Josephus and Roman records do not corroborate Luke's claims; Luke may have conflated the AD 6/7 census with Jesus' birth narrative; the theological motive is clear — getting Jesus to Bethlehem fulfills Micah 5:2; ancient historians regularly used historical frameworks for theological purposes.
positions.weaknesses: Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence; Luke's track record of accuracy in Acts should extend some credibility; dismissing the account as fabrication assumes Luke was either ignorant or dishonest; the theological motive does not preclude historical foundations.

synthesis: Defenders of historicity propose various solutions: Quirinius may have served twice in Syria, the census may have been conducted in stages, or the Greek prōtē ('first') may mean 'before' (i.e., 'before the census under Quirinius'). Critics argue Luke constructed the census to get Jesus from Nazareth to Bethlehem for theological reasons (Micah 5:2). The evidence is genuinely ambiguous — neither strong enough to confirm nor to definitively refute Luke's account. Luke's general historical reliability elsewhere (confirmed by archaeology for Acts) provides a basis for extending benefit of the doubt.

positions.argument: Christological

title: The "Sign of Jonah": Preaching or Resurrection?
question: The "Sign of Jonah": Preaching or Resurrection?

positions.label: Luke's emphasis: the preacher
positions.argument: Luke's version focuses on Jonah as a preacher — his whole mission to Nineveh is the sign. The Son of Man's preached word will be the sign to this generation.

positions.label: Matthew's emphasis: the resurrection
positions.argument: Matthew (12:40) specifies the three days in the fish as a type of the resurrection. The sign is death and vindication.

title: The Lord's Prayer: One Form or Two?
question: The Lord's Prayer: One Form or Two?

positions.label: Luke preserves the original
positions.argument: Luke's shorter form is likely closer to the original. Matthew expanded it for liturgical use — the additions are consistent with his other expansions of Q material.

positions.label: Matthew preserves the original
positions.argument: Matthew's fuller form may reflect a carefully transmitted liturgical tradition from the Jerusalem community.

title: Does 12:49 refer to the fire of the Spirit or the fire of judgment?
question: Does 12:49 refer to the fire of the Spirit or the fire of judgment?

positions.label: Fire of the Spirit
positions.argument: The fire Jesus came to cast (v.49) is the fire of the Holy Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2:3-4). The "baptism" of v.50 and the Spirit's fire are two sides of the same event.

positions.label: Fire of judgment and division
positions.argument: The fire is the eschatological judgment that Jesus's arrival initiates — consistent with John the Baptist's fire-and-Spirit imagery (3:16-17) and the division language of vv.51-53.

title: Is 12:47-48 teaching degrees of punishment in hell?
question: Is 12:47-48 teaching degrees of punishment in hell?

positions.label: Yes, graduated eschatological punishment
positions.argument: The passage implies that punishment in the final judgment is proportionate to knowledge and responsibility — a teaching consistent with Rom 2:12-16 and Heb 10:29.

positions.label: Contextual hyperbole about earthly discipline
positions.argument: The punishment language refers to the consequences of unfaithfulness in this life and in the community, not necessarily to eternal states.

title: Does 13:35 refer to the Palm Sunday entry or to the Parousia?
question: Does 13:35 refer to the Palm Sunday entry or to the Parousia?

positions.label: Palm Sunday
positions.argument: The immediate narrative context supports a near-term reference: 19:38 records the crowd's "Blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord!" as Jesus enters Jerusalem. The saying is fulfilled within the same travel narrative.

positions.label: The Parousia
positions.argument: The full recognition implied — Jerusalem "seeing" Jesus with the Hallel acclamation — was not achieved at the Palm Sunday entry, where Jesus was rejected within days. The ultimate fulfilment is eschatological.

title: The Great Banquet: Israel and the Gentiles?
question: The Great Banquet: Israel and the Gentiles?

positions.label: Three-stage allegorical reading
positions.argument: The three invitation stages correspond to: (1) the religious establishment's refusal; (2) inclusion of tax collectors and sinners within Israel; (3) the Gentile mission. The parable encodes the history of the early church's mission.

positions.label: Social criticism without allegory
positions.argument: The parable is primarily a subversive social critique of elite reciprocity culture, not an allegory of salvation history. The allegorical reading domesticates what is a more radical social challenge.

title: Is the Elder Son Condemned or Invited?
question: Is the Elder Son Condemned or Invited?

positions.label: Elder son condemned
positions.argument: The elder's resentment represents a fundamental failure of love that the parable exposes as disqualifying. He is the Pharisees; his exclusion from the feast is his own choice and its consequences are permanent.

positions.label: Elder son invited, not condemned
positions.argument: The parable does not end with the elder son refusing. The father goes out and pleads — as he went out to the younger. The open ending is an invitation: the Pharisees can still enter. The parable is evangelistic toward the elder son, not merely critical.

title: Is the Parable Literal Theology or Narrative Illustration?
question: Is the Parable Literal Theology or Narrative Illustration?

positions.label: Literal afterlife geography
positions.argument: The parable describes actual conditions in the intermediate state: conscious existence, recognition, memory, the chasm, and the finality of post-death conditions. Jesus is teaching doctrine through narrative.

positions.label: Narrative illustration using conventional imagery
positions.argument: Jesus uses the conventional Jewish imagery of Hades (from 1 Enoch, rabbinic tradition) to illustrate his point about wealth and poverty. The point is the reversal and the sufficiency of Scripture, not a map of the afterlife.

title: "The kingdom of God is in your midst" or "within you"?
question: "The kingdom of God is in your midst" or "within you"?

positions.label: Within you (interior)
positions.argument: Entos normally means "within/inside." The kingdom is an interior spiritual reality present in the hearts of those who receive it. This reading shaped centuries of Christian spirituality.

positions.label: In your midst (among you)
positions.proponents: Most modern scholars: Fitzmyer, Green, Marshall, Bock
positions.argument: Jesus would not say the kingdom is "within" the Pharisees who oppose him. Luke consistently presents the kingdom as present in Jesus's ministry and person. Entos can mean "among" (Ps 109:30 LXX uses it this way).

title: Why does Jesus ask "Why do you call me good?" (18:19)?
question: Is Jesus denying his own goodness, testing the ruler's theology, or correcting flattery?
context: When the rich ruler addresses Jesus as 'Good Teacher,' Jesus responds: 'Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone' (Luke 18:19; cf. Mark 10:18). This response has been used by unitarians to deny Jesus' deity (he distinguishes himself from God) and by trinitarians to affirm it (he implies that calling him 'good' logically requires acknowledging his deity). The question is whether Jesus is making a christological point, a pastoral point, or a polemical point.

positions.label: Jesus denies his own goodness
positions.argument: Jesus is deflecting the title "good teacher" because he does not consider himself divine. This reading uses the saying to argue against early high Christology.
positions.strengths: The plain reading — Jesus distinguishes himself from the 'one who is good' (God); consistent with Jesus' subordination to the Father in the Synoptics; avoids reading later high Christology back into the historical Jesus.
positions.weaknesses: Contradicts the broader NT witness to Jesus' sinlessness (2 Cor 5:21; Heb 4:15; 1 Pet 2:22); Luke-Acts presents Jesus as uniquely righteous; if Jesus is not good, the gospel's soteriology collapses; the Synoptic Jesus elsewhere claims divine prerogatives.

positions.label: Jesus is testing the ruler's theology
positions.argument: Jesus is not denying his own goodness but pressing the ruler: do you understand what you're saying? "Good" belongs to God alone — so who do you think I am? The question is an invitation to Christological recognition, not a denial of divinity.
positions.strengths: Jesus is asking whether the ruler understands what calling him good implies — an implicit deity claim; the question forces the ruler to think about the relationship between Jesus and God; consistent with Jesus' Socratic teaching style; does not deny Jesus' goodness but challenges superficial usage.
positions.weaknesses: This reading may be motivated by dogmatic concerns rather than the text's surface meaning; the ruler's response shows no awareness of a christological test; the reading requires inferring an unstated implication.

positions.label: Jesus is correcting flattery
positions.argument: The ruler is using "Good teacher" as a social honorific. Jesus redirects: goodness is a divine attribute, not a teaching compliment. The saying is about theological seriousness, not Christology.
positions.strengths: The ruler uses 'Good Teacher' as a conventional flattering address; Jesus deflects human praise toward God, consistent with his teaching elsewhere; the point is ethical — don't call me good, obey the commandments; the simplest pastoral reading.
positions.weaknesses: Calling a rabbi 'good' was unusual, not mere flattery — it signals genuine respect; Jesus may be making a deeper point than correcting manners; the flattery reading underestimates the christological significance.

synthesis: Jesus is almost certainly not denying his own goodness — the broader context of Luke-Acts presents Jesus as sinless and divine. The most likely reading is that Jesus is challenging the ruler's casual use of 'good' — if you call me good, do you realize what that implies? The question functions as an implicit christological claim: only God is good, and you are right to call me good, but do you understand the full weight of what you are saying? The ruler's subsequent failure to follow Jesus suggests he did not.

title: Is Zacchaeus's statement in 19:8 a promise or a defence?
question: Is Zacchaeus's statement in 19:8 a promise or a defence?

positions.label: A promise: present conversion
positions.argument: Zacchaeus's present tense ("I give" / "I will pay back") represents a declaration of immediate intent — his spontaneous response to Jesus's presence. This reading makes the story a conversion narrative.

positions.label: A defence: describing current practice
positions.argument: Zacchaeus is defending himself against the crowd's accusation: "Look, I already do give half... and when I have defrauded someone, I already do repay fourfold." On this reading, the story is about unjust accusation, not conversion.

title: What is the "image of God" implication in 20:24-25?
question: What is the "image of God" implication in 20:24-25?

positions.label: Coin/tax only
positions.argument: Jesus is making a practical point about Roman taxation: coins bearing Caesar's image belong in Caesar's domain; taxes are therefore legitimate. The God-claim is about Temple tithes and religious obligations.

positions.label: Humans as God's image
positions.argument: Since humans bear God's image (Gen 1:26), the full answer to "give to God what is God's" is: give yourself — totally — to God. The eikōn wordplay is intentional and subversive: Caesar gets his coins; God gets everything that bears his image, which is you.

title: "This generation" in 21:32: Near or Far Fulfilment?
question: Does 'this generation will not pass away' in Luke 21:32 refer to AD 70, the end times, or something else?
context: In Luke's Olivet Discourse, Jesus says 'this generation will not pass away until all these things have happened' (21:32). The 'things' include the destruction of Jerusalem, cosmic signs, and the coming of the Son of Man. If 'this generation' means Jesus' contemporaries, then either all predictions were fulfilled by AD 70, or Jesus was wrong about the timeframe. If 'generation' means something broader (the Jewish people, humanity, the type of people who reject God), the temporal urgency is removed but the word's normal meaning is stretched.

positions.label: Entirely fulfilled in AD 70
positions.proponents: Preterist interpreters: N.T. Wright, R.C. Sproul
positions.argument: "This generation" refers to the generation alive in AD 30; "all these things" refers to the Jerusalem destruction of AD 70. Luke 21:20-24 describes the siege; the Parousia language (21:25-27) is cosmic imagery for a historical event.
positions.strengths: Luke's redaction emphasizes Jerusalem's fall (21:20-24 — 'when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies'); 'all these things' can refer to the destruction and its precursors; Jesus' contemporaries (genea) lived to see AD 70; avoids the failed-prophecy problem entirely.
positions.weaknesses: The cosmic signs (21:25-27) and the Son of Man's coming (21:27) do not have obvious AD 70 fulfillments; requires reading 'the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory' as a non-physical event; the discourse seems to anticipate the end of history, not just the end of the temple.

positions.label: Partially fulfilled in AD 70, fully at Parousia
positions.argument: The near events (AD 70) and far events (Parousia) are both in view; "this generation" may extend beyond a single human lifetime. A double fulfilment, with AD 70 as a type of the final end.
positions.strengths: The discourse has two horizons — near (Jerusalem's fall) and far (Parousia); 'this generation' refers to those who would witness the beginning of fulfillment (AD 70); the pattern of prophetic double fulfillment is well-attested in the OT; the most common evangelical reading.
positions.weaknesses: 'All these things' (panta) is comprehensive — it is difficult to split fulfillment across two events separated by millennia; the double-fulfillment model can seem like a convenient escape from the text's apparent meaning; Luke 21 does not clearly signal a two-horizon structure.

positions.label: "Generation" = the Jewish people
positions.argument: "This generation" (genea) means the Jewish people as an enduring race — they will persist until the final eschatological fulfilment.
positions.strengths: Genea can mean 'race' or 'kind' in some contexts; Jesus may be promising that the Jewish people will survive until the end — a remarkable prophecy given the diaspora; avoids the failed-prophecy problem while preserving an eschatological reading.
positions.weaknesses: Genea overwhelmingly means 'contemporaneous generation' in the Synoptics (Luke 7:31; 11:29-32; 17:25); this reading stretches the word beyond its normal range; appears motivated by the need to solve a theological problem rather than by lexical evidence.

synthesis: The AD 70 reading has strong support — Luke's version of the discourse is widely recognized as shaped by awareness of Jerusalem's destruction. The partial-fulfillment view (AD 70 + Parousia) is the most common evangelical position, treating the discourse as having a 'near' and 'far' horizon. The 'generation = race/type' reading avoids the failed-prophecy problem but lacks lexical support — genea almost always means a contemporaneous generation in the Synoptics.

title: Is Luke 22:43-44 (the angel and the bloody sweat) original?
question: Is Luke 22:43-44 (the angel and the bloody sweat) original?

positions.label: Original
positions.argument: The passage is present in many important early witnesses and absent mainly in Alexandrian manuscripts. Its omission may reflect early theological embarrassment about Christ's agony and its implications for his divinity. The stylistic features are consistent with Luke.

positions.label: Later addition
positions.argument: The best Alexandrian witnesses (p75, the corrector of Sinaiticus) omit it. Early Church Fathers sometimes seem unaware of it. The theological difficulty it presents may explain its original absence rather than subsequent omission.

title: Is "Father, forgive them" (23:34a) original?
question: Is "Father, forgive them" (23:34a) original?

positions.label: Original
positions.argument: Present in Sinaiticus, A, C, D and many others. Absent in p75, B, W and a few others. Internal evidence strongly favours inclusion: stylistic consistency with Luke, the prayer for persecutors echoed in Acts 7:60 (Stephen), and the obvious motive for early omission (anti-Jewish sentiment).

positions.label: Later addition
positions.proponents: Some scholars, Westcott-Hort originally excluded it
positions.argument: The best Alexandrian witnesses (p75, B) lack it. The motive for addition is clear (theological enhancement). The silence of some early Fathers may indicate non-awareness.

title: Does the Emmaus recognition at the breaking of bread presuppose a eucharistic setting?
question: Does the Emmaus recognition at the breaking of bread presuppose a eucharistic setting?

positions.label: Yes, eucharistic allusion is deliberate
positions.proponents: Fitzmyer, Green, most Catholic and many Protestant scholars
positions.argument: The sequence — took bread, gave thanks, broke it, gave it (24:30) — exactly parallels the Last Supper (22:19) and the feeding of the five thousand (9:16). Luke uses this four-verb pattern consistently for the Eucharist. The recognition at the breaking of bread is a model for Eucharistic presence of the risen Christ.

positions.label: Ordinary hospitality, not eucharistic
positions.argument: The disciples are guests at Emmaus, not officiants at a sacrament. The four-verb pattern is simply how meals are described. The theological point is about hospitality as the occasion of recognition, not about a specific sacramental theology.

title: Is the Prologue a Pre-existing Hymn?
question: Is the Prologue a Pre-existing Hymn?

positions.label: Yes: hymnic adaptation
positions.argument: The prologue has a poetic, rhythmic structure distinct from the surrounding narrative, with prose insertions about John the Baptist (vv.6-8, 15). The original hymn may have been a Johannine community hymn to the Logos that the evangelist adapted.

positions.label: No: unified composition
positions.argument: The supposed hymnic structure is not consistent enough to identify a discrete pre-existing hymn. The prologue is the evangelist's own introduction, written in elevated prose that reflects the content's grandeur.

title: One Temple Cleansing or Two?
question: One Temple Cleansing or Two?

positions.label: Two cleansings: one at start, one at end
positions.argument: The differences between John and the Synoptics (timing, details, context) are best explained by two separate events. Jesus's prophetic action would naturally provoke escalating hostility over three years.

positions.label: One cleansing: John moved it for theological reasons
positions.argument: John deliberately placed the cleansing at the beginning to introduce the temple-body theme early. Historical-critical scholarship generally prefers the Synoptic timing as more historically plausible and sees John's placement as theological redaction.

title: "Water and Spirit" in 3:5: Baptism or Regeneration?
question: When Jesus says one must be 'born of water and the Spirit' (John 3:5), does 'water' refer to baptism, spiritual cleansing, or physical birth?
context: Jesus tells Nicodemus, 'Unless someone is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God.' The meaning of 'water' is debated. Sacramental traditions identify it with baptism. Others read 'water and Spirit' as a hendiadys (one concept expressed in two words) meaning 'spiritual cleansing' (cf. Ezek 36:25-27). A third view takes 'water' as referring to physical birth ('breaking of waters') — supported by the context where Nicodemus asks about entering 'a second time into his mother's womb' (3:4).

positions.label: Water = baptism
positions.proponents: Catholic and many patristic interpreters, Luther
positions.argument: The natural reading of "water" in a baptism context (John 3 follows John's baptising ministry) is Christian baptism. The new birth is sacramentally enacted in baptism. Augustine, Chrysostom, and Luther all hold this view.
positions.strengths: The early church universally connected this verse with baptism; Titus 3:5 links 'washing of regeneration' with renewal by the Holy Spirit; John's Gospel emphasizes baptism (1:26-33; 3:22-26; 4:1-2); the most straightforward sacramental reading.
positions.weaknesses: Jesus is speaking to Nicodemus before Christian baptism exists; the OT background (Ezek 36:25-27) uses 'water' as a metaphor for cleansing, not as a reference to a rite; making baptism necessary for salvation creates theological difficulties with sola fide.

positions.label: Water = the Spirit's cleansing work (hendiadys)
positions.argument: Water and Spirit describe the same event — the Spirit's purifying regenerative work promised in Ezek 36:25-27. The OT background and the immediate context (Nicodemus doesn't know baptism yet) favour this reading.
positions.strengths: Ezekiel 36:25-27 promises God will 'sprinkle clean water' and give a 'new Spirit' — a single act of eschatological renewal, not two separate events; 'water and Spirit' is a hendiadys meaning 'the Spirit's cleansing work'; avoids making a ritual prerequisite for salvation.
positions.weaknesses: Hendiadys readings can be difficult to demonstrate conclusively; the two nouns ('water and Spirit') separated by 'and' (kai) are more naturally read as two distinct elements; may seem motivated by anti-sacramental theology.

positions.label: Water = physical birth
positions.argument: "Born of water" = amniotic fluid (natural birth); "born of Spirit" = spiritual birth. Jesus is distinguishing natural from spiritual birth.
positions.strengths: Fits Nicodemus' question about physical birth (3:4); 'born of water' = natural birth, 'born of Spirit' = spiritual birth; the contrast between flesh and Spirit (3:6) supports two types of birth; amniotic fluid ('waters breaking') is a natural metaphor for birth.
positions.weaknesses: 'Born of water' for physical birth has no parallel in ancient Jewish or Greek literature; the amniotic-fluid interpretation is a modern reading unknown to ancient commentators; reduces 'water' to a trivial reference when Jesus is making a profound theological point.

synthesis: Each reading has strong advocates. The baptismal reading dominated the early church and remains standard in Catholic, Orthodox, and many Protestant traditions. The hendiadys reading is favored by Reformed scholars who emphasize regeneration over sacrament. The physical-birth reading fits the immediate context (Nicodemus' question about physical birth) but has less support in the broader Johannine theology. Many scholars now see a deliberate polysemy — John's Gospel frequently uses words with multiple levels of meaning.

title: Is the Samaritan Woman a Historical Figure or a Symbol?
question: Is the Samaritan Woman a Historical Figure or a Symbol?

positions.label: Historical and symbolic
positions.argument: The encounter is historical (specific geographical details, personal details about the woman's life) but also carries symbolic resonance (five husbands = five foreign nations of 2 Kgs 17; the woman = Samaria as bride). Both levels operate simultaneously.

positions.label: Primarily symbolic
positions.argument: The woman represents Samaria seeking its true husband (God); the five husbands are the five gods imported by the five nations. The personal details are allegorical rather than biographical.

title: Realised vs. Future Eschatology in John 5
question: Realised vs. Future Eschatology in John 5

positions.label: Primarily realised
positions.argument: The emphasis in John is on present possession: "has eternal life," "has crossed over from death to life" (v.24). Future resurrection language (vv.28-29) may be a later ecclesiastical addition to John's original text.

positions.label: Both present and future, complementary
positions.argument: Both dimensions are authentically Johannine and authentically dominical. Present realisation of eternal life (v.24-25) and future bodily resurrection (v.28-29) are both affirmed; they describe different aspects of the same salvation. The Gospel maintains this both/and throughout.

title: Is John 6:51-58 Eucharistic?
question: Is Jesus' discourse about 'eating my flesh and drinking my blood' in John 6 a reference to the Eucharist?
context: In John 6:51-58, Jesus declares, 'Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you.' The language is strikingly physical. Catholic and Orthodox tradition reads this as a clear eucharistic reference — the most explicit NT teaching on the Real Presence. Protestant interpreters typically read it as a metaphor for believing in Jesus, supported by 6:35 ('whoever believes in me will never be thirsty') and 6:63 ('the flesh counts for nothing; the words I have spoken to you are spirit and life'). The Fourth Gospel notably omits the institution of the Lord's Supper at the Last Supper.

positions.label: Yes: reference to the Eucharist
positions.proponents: Catholic, Orthodox, many Protestant scholars (Brown, Hoskyns)
positions.argument: The language shifts to the physical trōgō; the flesh/blood pairing is the eucharistic formula; the synagogue setting doesn't preclude post-resurrection sacramental reference. John deliberately replaces the Last Supper institution with this discourse.
positions.strengths: The language is unmistakably physical — 'eat' (trōgō = 'chew/munch,' not just metaphorical eating); 'flesh and blood' echoes the institution words; John omits the Last Supper institution, suggesting the eucharistic teaching is placed here instead; the early church read it eucharistically.
positions.weaknesses: John 6:63 ('the flesh counts for nothing; the words I have spoken to you are spirit') seems to deflate a purely physical reading; Jesus is speaking to a crowd unfamiliar with the Eucharist; making the Eucharist necessary for eternal life creates soteriological difficulties.

positions.label: No: metaphorical reference to faith
positions.argument: Verse 63 ("the flesh counts for nothing; the Spirit gives life") interprets the eating as spiritual. The discourse is about faith (v.29, 35, 40, 47) throughout; the "flesh and blood" intensification is a figure for complete appropriation of Christ.
positions.strengths: John 6:35 defines the metaphor: 'whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty' — eating/drinking = coming/believing; 6:63 explicitly says 'the flesh counts for nothing'; the discourse is about Christological faith, not a sacramental rite.
positions.weaknesses: The shift to trōgō ('chew') in 6:54 seems deliberately physical, going beyond metaphor; the discourse caused disciples to leave (6:66), suggesting Jesus was saying something more shocking than 'believe in me'; the metaphorical reading may domesticate the text's scandal.

positions.label: Both: the discourse is about faith, sacramentally enacted in the Eucharist
positions.argument: The discourse addresses faith in Christ; the Eucharist is the visible enactment of that faith. The sacrament is the appointed means of the spiritual eating the discourse describes.
positions.strengths: The discourse operates on multiple levels — primarily about faith in Christ, but shaped by eucharistic language familiar to John's community; this is consistent with John's layered symbolism throughout the Gospel; honors both the christological and sacramental dimensions.
positions.weaknesses: The 'both' reading can appear evasive; may not satisfy either sacramentalists or memorialists; the two levels of meaning are difficult to hold in tension without privileging one.

synthesis: The debate hinges on genre and context. The sacramental reading notes that John replaces the institution narrative with this discourse — the eucharistic theology is placed earlier in the narrative rather than at the Last Supper. The metaphorical reading observes that John consistently interprets physical images spiritually (water=Spirit, bread=belief, light=revelation). The both/and approach is gaining ground: the discourse is fundamentally about faith in Christ, but the language is shaped by eucharistic practice in the Johannine community.

title: Where do the "rivers of living water" flow from — the believer or Jesus?
question: Where do the "rivers of living water" flow from — the believer or Jesus?

positions.label: From the believer
positions.argument: "Whoever believes in me, as Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from within them." The believer becomes a source of life for others through the indwelling Spirit.

positions.label: From Jesus
positions.proponents: Some scholars (Origen, some modern interpreters), supported by punctuation
positions.argument: Punctuate vv.37-38 as: "Let anyone who is thirsty come to me, and let the one who believes in me drink. As Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from within him [Jesus]." The "him" refers to Jesus; the Spirit flows from the glorified Christ.

title: Does "before Abraham was, I am" assert eternal deity or simply pre-existence?
question: Does "before Abraham was, I am" assert eternal deity or simply pre-existence?

positions.label: Full divine name claim — eternal deity
positions.proponents: Most evangelical scholars, MacArthur, Carson, Keener
positions.argument: The egō eimi without predicate, the deliberate grammatical contrast (aorist genesthai vs present eimi), and the immediate stoning attempt (for blasphemy) all confirm a divine name claim. This is not merely pre-existence but eternal self-existent being.

positions.label: Pre-existence, not necessarily full deity
positions.argument: The statement asserts Jesus existed before Abraham; the "I am" is the natural Greek form for "I exist." The stoning may be for the whole discourse, not this specific phrase. This reading requires underweighting both the grammar and the opponents' response.

title: What is the purpose of the man's congenital blindness?
question: Does John 9:3 teach that God caused a man's blindness from birth to display divine power?
context: The disciples ask Jesus whether the man was born blind because of his own sin or his parents' sin (John 9:2). Jesus answers, 'Neither this man nor his parents sinned, but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him' (9:3). The 'so that' (hina) clause has troubled readers: does it mean God caused the blindness for the purpose of later healing it? This raises acute theodicy questions about divine causation of suffering. Alternative readings propose that Jesus is redirecting the question — not explaining the cause of blindness but pointing to its redemptive potential.

positions.label: Divine purpose: revelation
positions.argument: God ordained the blindness for this purpose: that the works of God might be displayed. This does not make God the author of evil but means that God in his sovereignty uses suffering for redemptive ends.
positions.strengths: Takes the hina ('so that') clause at face value as a purpose clause; parallels John 11:4 ('this sickness is for the glory of God'); God's sovereignty over all things — including suffering — is a consistent biblical theme (Exod 4:11); the healing reveals Jesus as the light of the world.
positions.weaknesses: Implies God caused decades of blindness merely to stage a healing; raises acute theodicy problems — does God deliberately inflict suffering to display power? May be pastorally harmful if applied to all suffering.

positions.label: Natural misfortune repurposed
positions.argument: Jesus is not saying God caused the blindness but that God can use the blindness. The "so that" is consequential rather than purposive: given the blindness, the works of God will be displayed. This reading softens the providential claim.
positions.strengths: Jesus may be redirecting the question, not answering it causally — 'but rather [let us focus on the fact] that the works of God might be displayed'; the blindness is not God-caused but God-redeemed; avoids making God the author of suffering; the Greek allows a result clause rather than a purpose clause.
positions.weaknesses: The hina clause most naturally reads as purpose, not result; this interpretation may be motivated by discomfort with divine sovereignty over suffering rather than by the grammar; John typically uses hina as a purpose clause.

positions.label: Eschatological purpose only
positions.argument: The man represents "the world" generically; the healing represents the eschatological restoration. The narrative is primarily symbolic rather than historical.
positions.strengths: The healing is a messianic sign — Isaiah 35:5 promises 'then will the eyes of the blind be opened'; the purpose is not to explain past suffering but to reveal the eschatological present; 'the works of God' point to the dawning kingdom, not to a theodicy.
positions.weaknesses: Does not fully address the cause of the blindness; the eschatological reading is theologically elegant but may sidestep the text's harder implications; the disciples' question demands some kind of answer.

synthesis: The divine-purpose reading takes the hina clause at face value but need not imply that God cruelly imposed blindness — God's sovereignty encompasses suffering and redemption together. The repurposing reading avoids making God the direct cause of suffering while affirming that God can bring good from it. The eschatological reading emphasizes that the healing is a sign of the messianic age. Most Johannine scholars recognize that the narrative's primary function is christological (Jesus as light of the world, 9:5) rather than philosophical (explaining the origin of suffering).

title: "I and the Father are one" — unity of essence or unity of purpose?
question: Does Jesus' claim 'I and the Father are one' (John 10:30) assert ontological unity or functional agreement?
context: John 10:30 is one of the clearest christological declarations in the Gospels. The Jewish audience understands it as a claim to deity and takes up stones (10:31). The verse was central to the Arian controversy — Arius argued for unity of will, while Athanasius argued for unity of essence (homoousios). The neuter hen ('one thing') rather than masculine heis ('one person') has been parsed both ways: it either indicates a shared divine nature without collapsing the persons, or it indicates agreement in purpose while preserving the Father's superiority.

positions.label: Unity of essence/nature (orthodox Trinitarianism)
positions.proponents: Athanasius, Calvin, MacArthur, most orthodox interpreters
positions.argument: The neuter hen asserts a unity of divine substance. The opponents' immediate response (stoning for blasphemy, "you claim to be God," v.33) confirms a divine nature claim. Jesus does not deny the charge; he defends the claim's consistency with Scripture.
positions.strengths: The Jewish audience understood it as a deity claim and attempted stoning for blasphemy (10:31-33); John 5:17-18 confirms that 'making himself equal with God' is the charge; the neuter hen ('one thing') indicates shared nature without personal identity; affirmed by Nicea, Chalcedon, and virtually all Christian traditions.
positions.weaknesses: The neuter hen could indicate agreement rather than identity; John 17:21-22 uses similar 'one' language for believers' unity with God, which cannot be ontological; the subordination texts (14:28 — 'the Father is greater than I') create tension.

positions.label: Unity of purpose/will only
positions.proponents: Arianism, Jehovah's Witnesses, some liberal scholars
positions.argument: Hen refers to unity of purpose and mission, not ontological identity. The Father and Son work together as one. This reading cannot account for the stoning response or for John's prologue ("the Word was God").
positions.strengths: The neuter hen ('one') rather than masculine heis ('one person') suggests unity of purpose, not identity; John 17:21-22 uses the same language for believers' unity — clearly not ontological; the Son always acts in obedience to the Father (5:19, 8:28), suggesting functional subordination, not essential equality.
positions.weaknesses: Does not adequately explain why the Jews attempt to stone Jesus for blasphemy — functional agreement would not be blasphemous; John 1:1 ('the Word was God'), 5:18 ('making himself equal with God'), and 20:28 ('My Lord and my God') collectively demand more than purpose-unity; this was Arius's position, condemned at Nicea.

positions.label: Both — inseparable
positions.argument: The unity of purpose (everything the Son does the Father does, ch.5) and the unity of essence are not separable in John's theology. The hen statement encompasses both, grounded in the ontological.
positions.strengths: Unity of essence grounds unity of purpose — the Son does what the Father does because the Son shares the Father's nature; the Johannine Father-Son relationship encompasses both ontological and functional dimensions; this is the classical trinitarian reading that avoids reducing the claim to either pure ontology or mere agreement.
positions.weaknesses: The 'both' reading is theologically sophisticated but may seem to collapse a genuine exegetical question into a systematic-theological synthesis; the text itself may be making a specific claim rather than a comprehensive trinitarian statement.

synthesis: The Nicene consensus, affirmed by the vast majority of Christian traditions, reads John 10:30 as asserting unity of essence — the Son shares the Father's divine nature. The Jewish audience's reaction (attempting to stone Jesus for blasphemy) confirms they understood the claim as more than functional agreement. The unity-of-purpose reading, championed by Arius and modern unitarians, does not adequately explain the blasphemy charge. However, the Nicene reading also affirms that the Son is not the Father — the neuter hen preserves personal distinction within essential unity.

title: Why did Jesus weep if he knew he was about to raise Lazarus?
question: Why does Jesus weep at Lazarus' tomb if he knows he is about to raise him from the dead?
context: John 11:35 — 'Jesus wept' — is the shortest verse in the English Bible but one of the most theologically significant. Jesus has already told the disciples that Lazarus' sickness 'will not end in death' (11:4) and that he will 'wake him up' (11:11). Yet when he arrives and sees Mary weeping, he is 'deeply moved' (enebrimēsato — literally 'snorted with indignation') and weeps. The Greek embrimaomai suggests anger or agitation more than sorrow. Why does Jesus display such emotion when the resurrection is minutes away?

positions.label: Genuine solidarity with human grief
positions.argument: Jesus wept because death is genuinely grievous. His foreknowledge of the resurrection does not negate the real grief of the moment; it contextualises it. He weeps for Mary and Martha and for the condition of humanity under death, even as he is about to overcome it.
positions.strengths: Demonstrates the full humanity of Jesus — he does not merely simulate emotion; Hebrews 4:15 affirms Jesus was 'tempted in every way, just as we are'; the incarnation means truly sharing human experience including grief; 'Jesus wept' reveals God's compassion for human suffering.
positions.weaknesses: If Jesus knows the resurrection is imminent, the grief seems performative; the Greek embrimaomai in 11:33 suggests anger more than sadness; the tears may be about something deeper than simple grief.

positions.label: Anger at death's power
positions.proponents: Some scholars reading embrimaomai as indignation
positions.argument: The groaning in v.33 (embrimaomai) is an expression of righteous anger at death and the evil that produces it. The tears are the overflow of that anger. Jesus weeps not as a victim of grief but as the conqueror of death who is angry at its domain.
positions.strengths: embrimaomai ('deeply moved') carries connotations of anger or indignation in classical Greek; Jesus is angry at death itself — the enemy he came to destroy; the weeping is not sentimental but combative; this explains why the emotion precedes the miracle — the anger fuels the command 'Lazarus, come out!'
positions.weaknesses: Anger at death does not fully explain the tears; the bystanders interpret the weeping as love for Lazarus (11:36), not as anger; may read too much into the lexical range of embrimaomai.

positions.label: Both
positions.argument: The weeping is both genuine grief (solidarity with the sisters) and something harder to name — perhaps the weight of what is being asked of him, the shadow of his own coming death over the scene.
positions.strengths: Human emotions are complex — grief and anger coexist naturally; Jesus weeps with Mary (solidarity) and is indignant at death (cosmic combat); the incarnation means experiencing the full range of human emotion; this reading honors the text's emotional complexity.
positions.weaknesses: The 'both' reading can appear evasive; the narrative may intend to foreground one emotion over the other; systematic readings risk flattening the text's dramatic tension.

synthesis: The two emotions (grief and anger) likely coexist. Jesus grieves with genuine human solidarity — the incarnation means truly entering human suffering. He is also indignant at death's power over those he loves. The tears are not a sign of doubt or helplessness but of the Incarnate God's full engagement with the world's brokenness. John presents a Jesus who is both the sovereign Lord who will raise Lazarus and the compassionate friend who weeps with those who mourn.

title: Does "I will draw all people" teach universal salvation?
question: Does "I will draw all people" teach universal salvation?

positions.label: No: "all" means all kinds / all the elect
positions.argument: "All" (pantas) in John often means "all without distinction" rather than "all without exception" (cf. 1:7; 3:26; 4:25; 11:48). The context (Gentiles as well as Jews) supports "all peoples/all nations." The drawing is effective for those drawn.

positions.label: Yes: universal atonement scope, not universal salvation
positions.argument: "All people" means every individual — the cross provides sufficient atonement for all. Whether all are saved depends on their response. The drawing is real but resistible (6:44 shows that drawing is necessary; it does not show it is irresistible).

positions.label: Universal salvation (apokatastasis)
positions.argument: The universal drawing combined with the universal love (3:16) and Paul's "all in all" (1 Cor 15:28) teaches the eventual salvation of all. Most mainstream scholars regard this as reading too much into any single passage.

title: Is the foot-washing in John 13 a sacrament?
question: Is the foot-washing in John 13 a sacrament?

positions.label: Yes: an ordinance / third sacrament
positions.proponents: Some Free Church traditions, Anabaptists, certain Pentecostals
positions.argument: "Unless I wash you, you have no part with me" (v.8) gives the washing a necessity that parallels baptism. Some traditions practice foot-washing as a required ordinance alongside baptism and the Lord's Supper.

positions.label: No: an example only
positions.argument: The command in v.14-15 is exemplary, not sacramental. The washing illustrates the principle of servant-love; it does not constitute a sacrament. John does not present it with the covenantal framework given to baptism or the Lord's Supper.

positions.label: Both exemplary and spiritually significant
positions.argument: The washing carries both moral (service) and spiritual (cleansing) dimensions without being a full sacrament in the technical sense. Its washing language (v.8: "unless I wash you") links it to baptism without identifying it with baptism.

title: "The Father is greater than I" — economic or ontological subordination?
question: "The Father is greater than I" — economic or ontological subordination?

positions.label: Economic subordination (incarnation only)
positions.argument: The Father's greatness refers to the Son's voluntary submission in the Incarnation. In the eternal relations, the Son is equal to the Father (1:1; 10:30); in the incarnate mission, the Son is functionally subordinate. The statement belongs to the economy, not the ontology.

positions.label: Eternal ontological subordination
positions.argument: The Father is ontologically greater — the Son is the first creation or a lesser divine being. The statement reflects the eternal order of being.

positions.label: Relational/personal subordination within equal essence
positions.argument: The eternal relations within the Trinity include a permanent relational order (Father-Son-Spirit) that involves authority/submission without ontological inequality. The Son eternally submits to the Father's authority while sharing the same divine nature.

title: What happens to branches that are "cut off" in 15:2, 6?
question: What happens to branches that are "cut off" in 15:2, 6?

positions.label: Genuine believers who fall away (arminian)
positions.argument: The branches are genuinely "in" Christ — truly saved — but can be cut off through persistent unfruitfulness or apostasy. The warning is genuine and addressed to real believers.

positions.label: False professors who were never truly saved (calvinist)
positions.argument: The fruitless branches had an external connection to Jesus (in the covenant community, in the church) but were never genuinely regenerate. Their removal reveals that they were never truly his (cf. 1 John 2:19).

positions.label: Loss of reward, not salvation (some dispensationalists)
positions.argument: The branches represent the believer's works, not the believer's person. Unfruitful branches lose rewards at the judgment but not salvation.

title: What does "I have overcome the world" mean?
question: What does "I have overcome the world" mean?

positions.label: Completed victory: the cross as the decisive defeat of evil
positions.proponents: Aulen (Christus Victor), most evangelical and orthodox scholars
positions.argument: The perfect tense declares a completed victory. The cross and resurrection constitute the decisive defeat of the world-system, Satan, sin, and death. The victory is ontologically complete; its full manifestation awaits the parousia.

positions.label: Moral victory: Jesus's faithfulness under pressure
positions.argument: "Overcoming the world" means maintaining integrity and love despite the world's opposition — a moral example rather than a cosmic event. This reading underweights both the perfect tense and the cosmic scope of "world" (kosmos) in John.

positions.label: Eschatological victory: not yet completed
positions.argument: The victory is proleptic — declared in anticipation of the final eschatological completion. It is certain but not yet fully realised.

title: What kind of unity does Jesus pray for in 17:21?
question: What kind of unity does Jesus pray for in 17:21?

positions.label: Spiritual/invisible unity of genuine believers
positions.argument: The unity is the organic unity of those who share the same divine life in Christ. It is invisible in the sense that only God can see it fully, but it produces visible fruits (love, shared mission, mutual service).

positions.label: Visible institutional unity of all Christian denominations
positions.proponents: Catholic, Orthodox, and ecumenical Protestant scholars
positions.argument: The purpose clause ("that the world may believe") requires visible unity. Invisible spiritual unity that leaves the church fragmented into competing denominations does not serve the missiological purpose of the prayer.

positions.label: Both, held in tension
positions.argument: Genuine spiritual unity (sharing the same life in Christ) must be expressed in visible ways (cooperation, mutual recognition, shared witness) to fulfil the prayer's missiological purpose. Neither invisible unity alone nor institutional merger alone satisfies the prayer.

title: Does "my kingdom is not of this world" teach political quietism?
question: Does "my kingdom is not of this world" teach political quietism?

positions.label: Yes: the church should not engage in political power
positions.argument: "Not of this world" means Jesus's kingdom operates by entirely different means than earthly kingdoms. The church that seeks political power has misunderstood its king.

positions.label: No: the kingdom engages the world by different means
positions.proponents: Most mainstream Catholic, Reformed, and evangelical scholars
positions.argument: "Not of this world" refers to origin and method, not location or relevance. The kingdom is present in and working through the world — through truth-bearing, justice-seeking, mercy-giving — but by the Spirit's power rather than coercion. The church engages political life by Christ's means: truth, love, and service.

positions.label: The kingdom will eventually transform political structures
positions.proponents: Post-millennial, Kuyperian Reformed, Catholic social teaching
positions.argument: The kingdom's "not-of-this-world" character does not prevent it from reshaping earthly institutions over time through the Spirit's work in history.

title: What do "blood and water" symbolise in 19:34?
question: What do "blood and water" symbolise in 19:34?

positions.label: Medical: physical confirmation of death
positions.proponents: Most medical scholars, many NT commentators
positions.argument: The blood and water are the physiological result of the spear-thrust — a pericardial effusion. The detail confirms the physical reality of the death against any swoon theory. The eyewitness emphasis (v.35) suggests John knew this detail would be challenged.

positions.label: Sacramental: baptism and Eucharist
positions.proponents: Augustine, most Catholic and Orthodox interpreters
positions.argument: Water = baptism; blood = Eucharist. The two sacraments flow from Christ's side as Eve came from Adam's side — the church born from the cross. Chrysostom: "From the side of Christ was born the church, as Eve from the side of the sleeping Adam."

positions.label: Johannine symbolic: Spirit and atonement
positions.argument: In John's own symbolism: water = the Spirit (7:38-39, given after glorification); blood = the atoning death (1:29; 6:53-56). The blood and water testify together to the completed atonement and the forthcoming gift of the Spirit.

title: Is John 20:22 the same event as Pentecost in Acts 2?
question: Is John 20:22 the same event as Pentecost in Acts 2?

positions.label: Different events: proleptic gift then full gift
positions.argument: John 20:22 is a preliminary or anticipatory gift of the Spirit — preparing the disciples for the mission Jesus assigns. Pentecost (Acts 2) is the full empowerment for the worldwide mission. The same Spirit is given in two stages or dimensions.

positions.label: Same event: two descriptions
positions.proponents: Some scholars (Barrett, Carson argues this is possible)
positions.argument: John and Luke describe the same Easter-Pentecost event. John telescopes Pentecost into Easter evening; Luke separates them. The theological meaning is the same.

positions.label: Symbolic gift: John 20:22 is a performed parable
positions.argument: The "breathing" is a symbolic anticipation of the Spirit's coming, not an actual impartation. The disciples don't receive the Spirit until Acts 2.

title: Is John 21 original to the Gospel or a later addition?
question: Is John 21 original to the Gospel or a later addition?

positions.label: A later addition by the same author or a disciple
positions.argument: Chapter 20:30-31 reads as a conclusion; chapter 21 is an appendix. The different location (Galilee vs Jerusalem) and the note about the beloved disciple's death rumour (v.23) suggest it was added after the author's death, possibly by the Johannine community. The style is consistent with the rest of the Gospel.

positions.label: Original, integral part of the Gospel
positions.argument: No manuscript of John lacks ch.21; it was always part of the Gospel. Chapter 20:30-31 is not a "conclusion" but a purpose statement that can appear at the end of a major section (as Paul puts purpose statements mid-letter). Chapter 21 provides the necessary rehabilitation of Peter and the identification of the author.

positions.label: Added by the same author as an epilogue
positions.argument: The style is authentically Johannine; the content (Peter's restoration, author identification) is necessary for the Gospel's completeness. The author added it after the main body was complete, perhaps after the death of the beloved disciple prompted the clarification of v.23.

title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough
question: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.label: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.label: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.
